title: Windows Setup Guide
text:
Path
Best for
Limitation




Git Bash
Quick start, no admin rights needed
Slightly different from macOS/Linux


WSL2
Full Linux environment inside Windows
Requires admin rights, 10–30 min setup



For this course, Git Bash is sufficient for all exercises. WSL2 is recommended if you plan to run Linux research tools (Python environments, bash scripts) on Windows.

Git Bash (Recommended Start)WSL2 (Full Linux)




Go to git-scm.com/download/win and download the latest installer (64-bit).



Accept defaults with these exceptions:

Default editor: Change from Vim to Nano (easier for beginners)
Adjusting PATH: Select “Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software”
Line endings: Select “Checkout as-is, commit as-is” (recommended for research files)




Search “Git Bash” in the Start menu. You should see a terminal prompt:
yourname@HOSTNAME MINGW64 ~
$



git --version
git version 2.44.0.windows.1



git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your@institution.edu"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
git config --global core.editor nano



ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@institution.edu"
Press Enter to accept default location (/c/Users/yourname/.ssh/id_ed25519).



cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
Copy the output. In GitHub: Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key → paste and save.



ssh -T git@github.com
Hi username! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.





Open PowerShell as Administrator and run:
wsl --install
This installs WSL2 with Ubuntu by default. Restart your computer when prompted.



After restart, Ubuntu opens automatically. Create a Unix username and password (these are separate from your Windows credentials).



In the Ubuntu terminal:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install git



git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your@institution.edu"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
git config --global core.editor nano



ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@institution.edu"
The key is stored at /home/yourname/.ssh/id_ed25519 inside WSL2.



cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
Copy and add to GitHub as described in the Git Bash tab.



ssh -T git@github.com
Hi username! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.

title: Windows Setup Guide
text:
Troubleshooting

ssh: connect to host github.com port 22: Connection refused

Your institution blocks port 22. Try SSH over HTTPS port 443:


ssh -T -p 443 git@ssh.github.com
If that works, add to ~/.ssh/config:
Host github.com
  Hostname ssh.github.com
  Port 443

Git Bash: $HOME path issues

Git Bash maps ~ to C:\Users\yourname. Use $USERPROFILE if a path does not resolve.

WSL2: wsl --install fails

You may need to enable virtualization in BIOS/UEFI. Check your laptop manufacturer’s documentation for enabling Intel VT-x or AMD-V.

No administrator rights

Use the Git Bash path — it does not require admin rights for installation.

title: Linux Setup Guide
text:
Troubleshooting

git: command not found

Installation may not have completed. Re-run the install command, or check echo $PATH to ensure /usr/bin is present.

Permission denied (publickey)

Run ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 to load your key into the agent, then retry.

Git version too old (< 2.28)

On older Ubuntu LTS releases, add the Git PPA: sudo add-apt-repository ppa:git-core/ppa && sudo apt update && sudo apt install git

title: Further Reading
text: Curated resources organized by topic. All free resources are marked with the source — many are available online at no cost.

title: Further Reading
text:
Git Internals
Pro Git (Scott Chacon & Ben Straub) The authoritative free book on Git, published by Apress and available at git-scm.com/book. Chapters 1–3 cover everything in this course. Chapters 10–11 (“Git Internals” and “Git and Other Systems”) provide the deeper object model and plumbing command coverage referenced in Git Internals Deep Dive.
Git from the Bottom Up (John Wiegley) A 40-page essay explaining Git by starting with object storage and working up to branches and merges — the inverse of how most tutorials teach it. Available free at jwiegley.github.io/git-from-the-bottom-up. Excellent complement to Module 01.
GitLab Git Reference Concise command reference with examples, maintained by GitLab. Useful as a secondary reference when GitHub’s documentation is platform-specific. → docs.gitlab.com/ee/topics/git

title: Further Reading
text:
Terminal Skills
The Linux Command Line (William E. Shotts) A comprehensive introduction to the Bash shell and Unix command-line tools, free online at linuxcommand.org/tlcl.php. Chapters 1–8 cover everything in Module 00 and more. Part 4 covers shell scripting (relevant to Module 11 hooks).
Bash Reference Manual The official GNU documentation for Bash. Dense but complete — useful when you need to understand shell syntax for writing hooks or aliases. → gnu.org/software/bash/manual
Terminal Cheat Sheet (SS64) Quick reference for commands on macOS (ss64.com/osx), Linux Bash (ss64.com/bash), and Windows CMD/PowerShell. Useful when looking up a specific command’s flags. → ss64.com

title: Further Reading
text:
Research Data Management
UK Data Service: Research Data Management Practical guidance for managing research data throughout the project lifecycle — data management plans, file naming, storage, access, and sharing. Especially relevant for clinical and health research. → ukdataservice.ac.uk/manage-data
FAIR Data Principles The FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) principles for scientific data management. The original 2016 paper is freely available: Wilkinson et al. (2016). Scientific Data, 3, 160018. → doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2016.18
Research Data Alliance (RDA): Active DMPs Working group resources on active data management plans that integrate version control and provenance tracking. Useful context for why Git fits into a broader data management strategy. → rd-alliance.org
OSF (Open Science Framework) A free platform for pre-registration, data sharing, and linking GitHub repositories to publications. Widely used in rehabilitation and health science research. → osf.io

title: Further Reading
text:
R Reproducibility
renv documentation Official documentation for the renv R package for reproducible environments. Covers the lock file format, snapshot/restore workflow, and integration with Git. → rstudio.github.io/renv
The Turing Way: Reproducible Research A community-maintained handbook covering version control, testing, continuous integration, and reproducible environments — with R and Python examples. Free online at the-turing-way.netlify.app. Particularly relevant: “Version Control” and “Reproducible Environments” chapters.
R for Reproducible Scientific Analysis (Software Carpentry) A workshop curriculum that teaches R, Git, and research best practices together. Free at swcarpentry.github.io/r-novice-gapminder. Useful companion to this course for learners who are new to R.

title: Further Reading
text:
Python Reproducibility
Python Packaging User Guide Official guide to pip, virtual environments, and requirements.txt — the Python equivalent of renv.lock. Essential for Python-based research projects. → packaging.python.org
nbstripout documentation Tool for clearing Jupyter notebook outputs before committing — covered briefly in Module 11. The README explains the Git filter mechanism in detail. → github.com/kynan/nbstripout
Jupytext documentation Alternative approach: sync .ipynb files to plain .py or .Rmd files for readable diffs in Git. Complements nbstripout for collaborative notebook workflows. → jupytext.readthedocs.io

title: Further Reading
text:
Rehabilitation Research Workflows
Stroke rehabilitation analysis on GitHub (example workflows) Several published rehabilitation studies include their analysis code on GitHub. Searching “stroke rehabilitation analysis R GitHub” or “physiotherapy outcome measure reproducible analysis” surfaces real examples of the patterns taught in this course. The rehab-research example repository used throughout this course follows these real-world conventions.
CONSORT statement and data transparency The CONSORT 2010 statement encourages transparency in reporting randomized trials. Linking a GitHub repository (tagged at the analysis version used for submission) to a trial registration is increasingly expected by high-impact journals. → consort-statement.org
EQUATOR Network: Reporting Guidelines A library of reporting guidelines for health research (CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, etc.). Each guideline increasingly expects or requires data and code availability — Git provides the version control infrastructure. → equator-network.org

title: Error Messages
text: A lookup reference for error messages you will encounter in Modules 00–12. Entries are organized by context. Use Ctrl+F / Cmd+F to search for the exact text of your error.

title: Error Messages
text:
Environment Setup

command not found: git
command not found: git
Meaning: Git is not installed, or its directory is not in your PATH.
Causes (most common first): 1. Git was never installed 2. Git was installed but the terminal session was not restarted after installation
Fix: - macOS: brew install git then open a new terminal - Linux: sudo apt install git or sudo dnf install git - Windows: Install Git for Windows from git-scm.com
→ Module 00, Setup Guides



ssh: connect to host github.com port 22: Connection refused
ssh: connect to host github.com port 22: Connection refused
Meaning: Port 22 (standard SSH) is blocked by a firewall.
Causes: 1. Institutional firewall blocks port 22 2. VPN or network policy restriction
Fix: Use SSH over port 443 (HTTPS port — usually allowed):
# Test if port 443 works
ssh -T -p 443 git@ssh.github.com

# Configure permanently (~/.ssh/config)
nano ~/.ssh/config
Add:
Host github.com
  Hostname ssh.github.com
  Port 443
→ Module 03



Permission denied (publickey)
git@github.com: Permission denied (publickey).
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.
Meaning: SSH authentication failed — GitHub rejected your key.
Causes (most common first): 1. Your SSH key is not loaded in the SSH agent 2. The public key in GitHub Settings does not match your ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub 3. You generated a key but never added it to GitHub
Fix:
# Load your key into the SSH agent
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Verify the loaded keys
ssh-add -l

# Re-test connection
ssh -T git@github.com
If still failing, copy cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub and verify it matches exactly what is in GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys.
→ Module 03, Exercise 3.1

title: Error Messages
text:
Staging & Committing

nothing to commit, working tree clean
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
Meaning: Not an error — this is the ideal state. No changes since the last commit.
→ Module 02



error: pathspec 'FILE' did not match any file(s) known to git
error: pathspec 'scripts/analysis.R' did not match any file(s) known to git
Meaning: The file path you specified does not match any tracked file.
Causes (most common first): 1. Typo in the file path 2. The file was deleted and is no longer in the working tree 3. Wrong directory — git commands must be run from inside the repository
Fix: Run git status to see what files exist. Run ls scripts/ to verify the file name. Run pwd to confirm you are inside the repository.
→ Module 02



Untracked files shown after adding to .gitignore
Untracked files:
    data/participants.csv
Even after adding data/ to .gitignore.
Meaning: The file was committed before .gitignore was set up. .gitignore only works on untracked files.
Fix: git rm --cached data/participants.csv then commit.
→ Module 08

title: Error Messages
text:
Pushing & Pulling

[rejected] main -> main (non-fast-forward)
 ! [rejected]        main -> main (non-fast-forward)
error: failed to push some refs to 'git@github.com:yourusername/repo.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not
hint: have locally. Integrate the remote changes (e.g.
hint: 'git pull --rebase ...') before pushing again.
Meaning: The remote has commits you do not have locally. Git refuses to overwrite them.
Causes: 1. A collaborator pushed while you were working 2. You used git commit --amend on a commit that was already pushed
Fix:
git pull --rebase origin main
git push origin main
Never use git push --force on a shared branch.
→ Module 03, Exercise 3.3



fatal: remote origin already exists
fatal: remote origin already exists.
Meaning: You already have a remote named origin.
Fix: Check existing remotes first:
git remote -v
If the URL is wrong, update it:
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:yourusername/repo.git
→ Module 03



fatal: 'origin' does not appear to be a git repository
fatal: 'origin' does not appear to be a git repository
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.
Meaning: The remote URL is incorrect or the repository does not exist on GitHub.
Causes: 1. Typo in the repository URL 2. The repository was deleted or renamed on GitHub 3. Wrong GitHub account
Fix:
git remote -v    # check the URL
gh repo view    # verify the repository exists (requires gh auth)
→ Module 03

title: Error Messages
text:
Merging

CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in FILE
Auto-merging scripts/analysis.R
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in scripts/analysis.R
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.
Meaning: Both branches modified the same lines. Git cannot choose automatically.
Causes: Two branches changed the same line(s) of the same file.
Fix:
# 1. See which files have conflicts
git status

# 2. Open the file and look for conflict markers
nano scripts/analysis.R

# 3. Delete the three marker lines, keep the content you want
# 4. Save

# 5. Stage the resolved file
git add scripts/analysis.R

# 6. Complete the merge
git merge --continue
→ Module 05, Exercise 5.1



You have unmerged paths.
On branch main
You have unmerged paths.
  (fix conflicts and run "git merge --continue")
  (use "git merge --abort" to abort the merge)

Unmerged paths:
  (use "git add <file>..." to mark resolution)
        both modified:   scripts/analysis.R
Meaning: You are in a merge conflict state. You must resolve all conflicts before the merge can complete.
Fix: Resolve each file listed under “Unmerged paths”, git add each one, then git merge --continue.
→ Module 05

title: Error Messages
text:
Rebasing

error: could not apply HASH... (rebase conflict)
error: could not apply 3c4d5e6... Fix: use z-score normalization
hint: Resolve all conflicts manually, mark them as resolved with
hint: "git add/rm <conflicted_files>", then run "git rebase --continue".
hint: You can instead skip this commit: run "git rebase --skip".
hint: To abort and get back to the state before "git rebase", run "git rebase --abort".
Meaning: A conflict occurred while replaying a commit during rebase.
Fix: Same resolution process as a merge conflict:
nano CONFLICTED_FILE     # resolve markers
git add CONFLICTED_FILE
git rebase --continue
→ Module 06



fatal: It seems that there is already a rebase-merge directory
fatal: It seems that there is already a rebase-merge directory, and
I wonder if you are in the middle of another rebase.  If that is the
case, please try
      git rebase (--continue | --abort | --skip)
Meaning: A previous rebase was not completed or aborted cleanly.
Fix: Check state and either continue or abort:
git status
git rebase --abort    # if you want to start fresh
→ Module 06

title: Error Messages
text:
Configuration

Please tell me who you are.
Author identity unknown

*** Please tell me who you are.

Run

  git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
  git config --global user.name "Your Name"
Meaning: Git cannot create a commit because no user identity is configured.
Fix: Run exactly what Git suggests:
git config --global user.email "your@institution.edu"
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
→ Module 01



error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by checkout
error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by checkout:
        scripts/analysis.R
Please commit your changes or stash them before you switch branches.
Aborting
Meaning: You have uncommitted changes that would be overwritten by the branch switch.
Causes: Unsaved working directory changes on a file that differs between branches.
Fix (choose one):
# Option 1: Commit your changes first
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "WIP: save in-progress changes"
git switch other-branch

# Option 2: Stash your changes
git stash save "WIP: changes before branch switch"
git switch other-branch
git stash pop    # restore after switching back
→ Module 04, Module 07



HEAD detached at HASH
HEAD detached at 3c4d5e6
nothing to commit, working tree clean
Meaning: You checked out a specific commit (or tag) directly. HEAD points to a hash, not a branch. Commits you make here are not on any branch.
Causes: git checkout HASH, git checkout TAG, or gh pr checkout N.
Fix:
# If you have not made any commits in detached state:
git switch main

# If you made commits you want to keep:
git switch -c rescue-branch    # attach HEAD to a new branch
→ Module 10, Exercise 10.1



fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git
fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git
Meaning: You ran a git command outside of a Git repository.
Fix: Navigate to your repository first:
cd ~/rehab-research
git status
→ Module 01



Filename too long (Windows)
error: could not create leading directories of '...' : No such file or directory
or
warning: Clone succeeded, but checkout failed.
Meaning: Windows has a 260-character path length limit by default. Long paths in the repository exceed this.
Fix: Enable long paths in Git:
git config --global core.longpaths true
Also enable in Windows Group Policy (requires admin): Computer Configuration → Administrative Templates → System → Filesystem → Enable Win32 long paths.
→ Setup Guides

title: Module 11: Git with Research Tools
text: Research workflows combine editors, statistical software, notebooks, and the terminal. This module shows how Git integrates with each tool — and why the terminal remains the authoritative interface regardless of which other tools you use.

title: Module 11: Git with Research Tools
text: Research workflows combine editors, statistical software, notebooks, and the terminal. This module shows how Git integrates with each tool — and why the terminal remains the authoritative interface regardless of which other tools you use.

title: Module 11: Git with Research Tools
text:
Prerequisites

Module 02 — Basic Workflow
Module 08 — Data Management (for the pre-commit hook section)



Git Terminal + RStudio: Recommended Workflow
RStudio includes a Git panel that provides buttons for common operations. Using it is tempting — but it hides critical context:







RStudio Git GUI
Terminal Git




“Commit” button
git commit -m "..." — message is explicit


Checkbox per file
git add scripts/analysis.R — files are explicit


No conflict markers
git status shows “both modified” clearly


No reflog access
git reflog — full recovery history visible


No rebase support
git rebase -i — history cleanup available



Recommended workflow: Use RStudio for writing and editing R code. Use the terminal for all Git operations. Open both windows side by side.

%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    ED["RStudio Editor\n(write R code)"] --> TERM["Terminal\n(git add, commit, push)"]
    TERM --> ED

    style ED fill:#bd93f9,color:#000
    style TERM fill:#50fa7b,color:#000










This way you always know exactly what Git is doing — no hidden operations.






If You Must Use the RStudio Git Panel



Open Tools → Terminal inside RStudio. This embeds a terminal directly in the IDE. You get the full terminal interface without switching windows, with files conveniently accessible in the Files pane.





renv + Git
renv manages R package versions for reproducibility. Configure it correctly with Git:
What to commit:
# renv.lock: YES — contains exact package versions for reproducibility
git add renv.lock
git commit -m "renv: lock package versions for Wave 1 analysis"

# .Rprofile: YES — activates renv when R starts in this directory
git add .Rprofile

# renv/settings.json: YES — renv configuration
git add renv/settings.json
What to exclude (add to .gitignore):
echo 'renv/library/' >> .gitignore
echo 'renv/staging/' >> .gitignore
renv/library/ contains the installed packages — thousands of files, platform-specific, and reproducible from renv.lock. Including it balloons the repository.
Verify renv.lock is being tracked:
git log --oneline renv.lock
b3a2c1d renv: lock package versions for Wave 1 analysis
9f0a1b2 renv: update tidyverse to 2.0.0
Restore environment on a new machine:
# Clone the repository
git clone git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
cd rehab-research

# Restore all packages from renv.lock
Rscript -e "renv::restore()"



Jupyter Notebooks and Git
Jupyter notebooks (.ipynb) store outputs — plots, tables, error messages — as JSON inside the file. This creates problems for Git:
The diff problem:
-   "execution_count": 7,
-   "outputs": [{"data": {"image/png": "iVBORw0KGgo...AAAA"}, ...}]
+   "execution_count": 8,
+   "outputs": [{"data": {"image/png": "iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhE...BBBB"}, ...}]
A re-run with identical code produces a different base64 image string — spurious diffs of thousands of lines on every commit.
Solution 1: nbstripout — clear outputs before commit
# Install
pip install nbstripout

# Configure for this repository (adds a Git filter)
nbstripout --install
Installed nbstripout in /Users/jbayer/rehab-research
Now every git commit automatically strips outputs from notebooks before storing them. The filter is recorded in .git/config (local) and .gitattributes (shared):
cat .gitattributes
*.ipynb filter=nbstripout
Commit .gitattributes so collaborators get the same filter:
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "Config: add nbstripout filter for Jupyter notebooks"
Solution 2: Jupytext — sync notebooks to plain Python
pip install jupytext

# Convert notebook to Python script (once)
jupytext analysis.ipynb --to py

# Sync automatically on save (add to .gitignore the .ipynb)
echo '*.ipynb' >> .gitignore
git add analysis.py
git commit -m "Add: analysis script (jupytext-synced from notebook)"
Plain .py files have clean, readable diffs. Collaborators can run jupytext analysis.py --to notebook to regenerate the notebook.



Pre-Commit Hooks via Shell Scripts
Git hooks are shell scripts that run at specific points in the Git workflow. A pre-commit hook runs before every commit — perfect for enforcing data safety rules.
Create a hook that blocks data file staging:
# Open the hook file
nano .git/hooks/pre-commit
Write this script:
#!/bin/bash
# Block commits that stage files in the data/ directory

staged_data=$(git diff --cached --name-only | grep -E "^data/")

if [ -n "$staged_data" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Staged file(s) found in data/ directory:"
    echo "$staged_data"
    echo ""
    echo "Add a data/ pattern to .gitignore and use 'git rm --cached' to unstage."
    exit 1
fi
Make it executable:
chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit
Test the hook:
# Try to commit a data file
echo "id,score" > data/test.csv
git add data/test.csv
git commit -m "Test: this should be blocked"
ERROR: Staged file(s) found in data/ directory:
data/test.csv

Add a data/ pattern to .gitignore and use 'git rm --cached' to unstage.
The commit is blocked. Fix:
git restore --staged data/test.csv
echo 'data/' >> .gitignore
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Data: add .gitignore to block data directory"






Hooks Are Local and Not Shared



Files in .git/hooks/ are not committed to the repository and do not push to remotes. Each collaborator must install hooks on their own machine.
To share hooks with your team, store the scripts in a tracked scripts/git-hooks/ directory and document the installation step in your repository’s README.





Git Aliases: Customize Your Workflow
Aliases create shortcuts for commands you type frequently:
# Short status
git config --global alias.st status

# One-line graph log (the most useful alias for researchers)
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --all --decorate"

# Unstage a file
git config --global alias.unstage "restore --staged"

# Show the last commit
git config --global alias.last "log -1 HEAD --stat"

# List all aliases
git config --global alias.aliases "config --get-regexp alias"
Verify the aliases are stored:
cat ~/.gitconfig
[user]
    name = Dr. J. Bayer
    email = j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu
[alias]
    st = status
    lg = log --oneline --graph --all --decorate
    unstage = restore --staged
    last = log -1 HEAD --stat
    aliases = config --get-regexp alias
Use them:
git st              # git status
git lg              # git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate
git unstage scripts/analysis.R   # git restore --staged scripts/analysis.R
git last            # show last commit with stats



Shell History as Documentation
Your terminal history is an implicit record of your Git activity:
history | grep "^[0-9]* *git"
  423  git add scripts/analysis.R
  424  git commit -m "Fix: z-score normalization"
  425  git log --oneline
  426  git switch feature/add-grip-strength-measure
  427  git merge main
Use this to reconstruct what you did during a session — useful when writing up methods sections or answering reviewer questions about the analysis history.
Search for specific git commands:
history | grep "git log"
history | grep "git reset"
For a permanent audit trail, enable timestamps in bash history:
echo 'export HISTTIMEFORMAT="%F %T "' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
Now history | grep git shows exact timestamps alongside each command.

title: Module 09: Collaboration via Terminal

title: Module 09: Collaboration via Terminal
text: When multiple researchers work on the same codebase, each maintains their own fork — a personal copy of the repository — and contributes changes back via pull requests (PRs). This module covers the complete forking workflow entirely from the terminal using the GitHub CLI (gh).

title: Module 09: Collaboration via Terminal
text:
Prerequisites

Module 05 — Merging and Conflicts
SSH authentication working (Exercise 3.1)
GitHub account



Forking Workflow Overview

%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    UP["upstream\n(shared repo)"] -->|fork| FORK["origin\n(your fork)"]
    FORK -->|clone| LOCAL["local\n(your machine)"]
    LOCAL -->|push| FORK
    FORK -->|pull request| UP
    UP -->|fetch upstream| LOCAL

    style UP fill:#50fa7b,color:#000
    style FORK fill:#bd93f9,color:#000
    style LOCAL fill:#8be9fd,color:#000










Three remotes in play: - upstream: The shared repository you do not directly write to - origin: Your personal fork on GitHub (you have write access) - local: Your working copy on your machine



GitHub CLI Setup
Install gh if not already available:

macOSLinuxWindows Git Bash


brew install gh


# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install gh

# Fedora/RHEL
sudo dnf install gh


Download the installer from cli.github.com and run it. gh is then available in Git Bash and PowerShell.



Authenticate:
gh auth login
? What account do you want to log into? GitHub.com
? What is your preferred protocol for Git operations on this host? SSH
? Upload your public key to your GitHub account? Yes
? Title for your SSH key: (GitHub CLI) GitHub CLI
✓ Authentication complete.
- gh config set -h github.com git_protocol ssh
✓ Configured git protocol
✓ SSH key already existed on your GitHub account: ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
✓ Logged in as yourusername
Verify:
gh auth status
github.com
  ✓ Logged in to github.com account yourusername (keyring)
  - Active account: true
  - Git operations protocol: ssh
  - Token: gho_****



Fork a Repository
gh repo fork OWNER/REPO --clone
Example — forking a practice repository:
gh repo fork rehab-institute/grip-strength-study --clone
✓ Created fork yourusername/grip-strength-study
Cloning into 'grip-strength-study'...
remote: Enumerating objects: 24, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (24/24), done.
Receiving objects: 100% (24/24), 4.32 KiB | 4.32 MiB/s, done.
✓ Cloned fork
! Repository rehab-institute/grip-strength-study set as the default repository. To learn more about the default repository, run: gh repo set-default --help
--clone forks and immediately clones in one step. The cloned directory is automatically configured with origin pointing to your fork.
Check remotes:
cd grip-strength-study
git remote -v
origin  git@github.com:yourusername/grip-strength-study.git (fetch)
origin  git@github.com:yourusername/grip-strength-study.git (push)
upstream    git@github.com:rehab-institute/grip-strength-study.git (fetch)
upstream    git@github.com:rehab-institute/grip-strength-study.git (push)
gh repo fork --clone automatically adds upstream for you.



Feature Branch Workflow
Always work on a branch — never commit directly to main on your fork:
# Create and switch to a feature branch
git switch -c feature/add-baseline-measures

# Make changes
nano scripts/analysis.R

# Stage and commit
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: baseline measure functions for grip and balance"

# Push to your fork
git push origin feature/add-baseline-measures
Enumerating objects: 5, done.
Counting objects: 100% (5/5), done.
Writing objects: 100% (3/3), 412 bytes | 412.00 KiB/s, done.
To git@github.com:yourusername/grip-strength-study.git
 * [new branch]      feature/add-baseline-measures -> feature/add-baseline-measures



Creating a Pull Request via Terminal
gh pr create \
  --title "Add: baseline measure functions for grip and balance" \
  --body "Implements measure_grip_strength() and normalize_balance() per protocol Section 3.2.

Resolves #12.

**Testing**: Verified output matches Table 2 in Smith et al. (2024)."
Creating pull request for yourusername:feature/add-baseline-measures into main in rehab-institute/grip-strength-study

https://github.com/rehab-institute/grip-strength-study/pull/47
The URL is the PR page on GitHub. Share it with your collaborator for review.
List open PRs:
gh pr list
Showing 2 of 2 open pull requests in rehab-institute/grip-strength-study

#47  Add: baseline measure functions  feature/add-baseline-measures  about 1 minute ago
#38  Docs: update wave 2 protocol     docs/wave2-protocol            3 days ago
Check status of your PR:
gh pr status
Relevant pull requests in rehab-institute/grip-strength-study

Current branch
  #47  Add: baseline measure functions  [open]
   - Checks: 1 passing
   - Review: 1 requesting changes



Reviewing Code via Terminal
Check out a collaborator’s PR to test it locally:
gh pr checkout 38
remote: Enumerating objects: 8, done.
Fetching pull request...
Switched to branch 'docs/wave2-protocol'
You are now on the PR’s branch. Review the changes:
git diff main..docs/wave2-protocol
Run their code, test it, then return to your branch:
git switch feature/add-baseline-measures
Add a review comment:
gh pr review 38 --comment --body "Looks good overall. One question: Table 3 references participants.csv — is that gitignored?"
Approve the PR:
gh pr review 38 --approve --body "LGTM — data exclusion criteria match protocol."



Keeping Your Fork Updated
When the upstream repository receives new commits, your fork falls behind. Sync it:
# Fetch all changes from upstream (does not modify your local branches)
git fetch upstream
remote: Enumerating objects: 8, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (8/8), done.
Receiving objects: 100% (5/5), 1.23 KiB | 1.23 MiB/s, done.
From git@github.com:rehab-institute/grip-strength-study
   3c4d5e6..7e8f9a0  main -> upstream/main
# View what's new
git log --oneline upstream/main
7e8f9a0 Fix: update inclusion criteria for Wave 2
3f2a1b4 Add: grip strength normative data
3c4d5e6 Initial commit
# Rebase your local main onto upstream/main (creates linear history)
git switch main
git rebase upstream/main
Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/main.
# Push the updated main to your fork
git push origin main
Total 0 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To git@github.com:yourusername/grip-strength-study.git
   3c4d5e6..7e8f9a0  main -> main
Your fork’s main now matches upstream. Feature branches built from main can now be rebased onto the updated main (see Module 06).






gh sync: One-Step Fork Sync



The GitHub CLI provides a shortcut:
gh repo sync
This fetches and merges upstream changes into your fork’s default branch automatically. It is convenient but merges rather than rebases — the manual git fetch upstream + git rebase upstream/main gives you more control.

title: Module 07: Advanced Git Operations
text: The commands you have learned so far cover 90% of daily Git use. This module covers the remaining operations researchers reach for when auditing their analysis history, recovering interrupted work, and preparing code for publication.

title: Module 07: Advanced Git Operations
text:
Prerequisites

Module 02 — Basic Workflow (git log, git diff)
Familiarity with branches helps but is not required for most sections



git show: Inspect a Commit
git show displays the full detail of any commit: author, date, message, and the complete diff of changes.
git show HEAD
commit 3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b1c2
Author: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   Mon Feb 28 14:23:11 2026 +0100

    Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index

    Changed from min-max to z-score normalization to match the
    published protocol (Smith et al., 2024, Table 2).

diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index a1b2c3d..4e5f6a7 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -3,4 +3,4 @@ library(tidyr)
-normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
+normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
Show a specific commit by hash:
git show 1a5f3b2
Show only the metadata (no diff):
git show --stat HEAD
commit 3c4d5e6...
Author: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   Mon Feb 28 14:23:11 2026 +0100

    Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index

 scripts/analysis.R | 2 +-
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)



Searching History: git log -S and –grep
Find commits that added or removed a specific string:
git log -S "normalize_balance"
commit 9d4c7e1a8b5c6d7e8f9a0b1c2d3e4f5a6b7c8d9
Author: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   Sat Feb 22 10:15:33 2026 +0100

    Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
-S (pickaxe search) finds commits where the string count changed — meaning it was added or removed. This answers: “when was this function introduced?”
Research use: Your balance scores are wrong. When was the normalization formula last changed?
git log -S "normalize_balance" --oneline
3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
Two commits touched normalize_balance. Inspect both with git show to understand the full chain of changes.
Find commits by message pattern:
git log --grep "Fix:" --oneline
3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
b2a3c4d Fix: correct normalization formula
--grep searches the commit message (not the code). Combine with -i for case-insensitive search:
git log --grep "normalization" -i --oneline
Combine search flags:
git log -S "normalize_balance" --grep "Fix:" --oneline
When multiple flags are given, Git returns commits matching ALL conditions (AND logic). Use --all-match explicitly if combining --grep multiple times.



git blame: Line-by-Line History
git blame shows the last commit to modify each line of a file:
git blame scripts/analysis.R
9d4c7e1a (Dr. J. Bayer     2026-02-22 10:15:33 +0100  1) library(dplyr)
9d4c7e1a (Dr. J. Bayer     2026-02-22 10:15:33 +0100  2) library(tidyr)
9d4c7e1a (Dr. J. Bayer     2026-02-22 10:15:33 +0100  3)
3c4d5e6f (Dr. J. Bayer     2026-02-28 14:23:11 +0100  4) normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
1a5f3b2c (M. Kwame          2026-02-25 09:42:07 +0100  5) filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))
Each line shows: hash (Author Date Time Zone LineNo) content
Research use: A statistical reviewer asks “who introduced the grip strength filter and when?” — git blame answers in seconds.
Show only a line range:
git blame -L 4,6 scripts/analysis.R
Show the original commit information (ignoring whitespace moves):
git blame -w scripts/analysis.R



Stashing: Save Work in Progress
Stash lets you save uncommitted changes temporarily — useful when you need to switch tasks without committing incomplete work.
Save current work:
# You are in the middle of editing analysis.R
git stash save "WIP: adding grip measure normalization"
Saved working directory and index state On feature/grip: WIP: adding grip measure normalization
Your working directory is now clean — the changes are stored in the stash stack.
List stashes:
git stash list
stash@{0}: On feature/grip: WIP: adding grip measure normalization
stash@{1}: On main: WIP: protocol section draft
Stashes are numbered from 0 (most recent).
Restore the most recent stash:
git stash pop
On branch feature/grip
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R

Dropped stash@{0} (3f2a1b4c5d6e7f8a9b0c1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a)
pop restores and removes the stash. Use apply to restore without removing:
git stash apply stash@{1}   # restore a specific stash without removing it
Remove a stash without applying:
git stash drop stash@{0}
Clear all stashes:
git stash clear






Stash Is Not a Backup



Stashes are stored locally in .git/refs/stash. They do not push to a remote. If you delete the repository, stashes are lost. For longer-term storage of experimental work, commit to a wip/ branch instead.





Tagging Releases: git tag
Tags mark specific commits as significant — typically version releases or manuscript submission points.
Create an annotated tag (recommended — stores author, date, and message):
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Analysis for Wave 1 manuscript submission"
List all tags:
git tag
v1.0.0
Inspect a tag:
git show v1.0.0
tag v1.0.0
Tagger: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 16:00:00 2026 +0100

Analysis for Wave 1 manuscript submission

commit 3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b1c2
Author: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 15:45:00 2026 +0100

    Polish: final cleanup before manuscript submission
...
Push tags to remote (tags do not push automatically):
git push origin v1.0.0        # push a single tag
git push origin --tags        # push all tags
Tag an older commit:
git tag -a v0.9.0 1a5f3b2 -m "Pre-submission analysis checkpoint"



Cherry-Picking: git cherry-pick
Cherry-pick copies a specific commit from one branch to another — useful when you need one fix without merging an entire branch.
Scenario: You fixed a bug on fix/normalize-balance-scores but main needs that fix now, before the entire fix branch is ready to merge.
# Find the commit hash of the fix
git log fix/normalize-balance-scores --oneline
7e8f9a0 Fix: correct sd() usage in normalize_balance
3c4d5e6 Add: initial normalize function
# Switch to the branch that needs the fix
git switch main

# Apply only that one commit
git cherry-pick 7e8f9a0
[main 9f0a1b2] Fix: correct sd() usage in normalize_balance
 Date: Mon Feb 28 11:22:33 2026 +0100
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
The fix is now on main with a new hash (same content, new parent). The original commit on fix/normalize-balance-scores is unchanged.






Cherry-pick Creates Duplicate Commits



When you eventually merge fix/normalize-balance-scores into main, the cherry-picked commit will appear twice in history with different hashes but identical content. This is cosmetically messy but functionally harmless. Prefer merging or rebasing when possible; use cherry-pick only for urgent targeted fixes.





Viewing File History: git log – filename
Show only commits that touched a specific file:
git log --oneline -- scripts/analysis.R
3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
Combine with -p to see the diff at each point:
git log -p -- scripts/analysis.R
Show renames (if the file was moved):
git log --follow --oneline -- scripts/analysis.R
--follow tracks renames across git mv operations.

title: Module 05: Merging and Conflicts via Terminal
text: Branching creates parallel lines of work. Merging brings them back together. Sometimes Git can merge automatically; sometimes the same lines were changed in both branches and you must choose which version to keep. This module teaches you to handle both cases entirely from the terminal.

title: Module 05: Merging and Conflicts via Terminal
text: Branching creates parallel lines of work. Merging brings them back together. Sometimes Git can merge automatically; sometimes the same lines were changed in both branches and you must choose which version to keep. This module teaches you to handle both cases entirely from the terminal.
crumbs: Module 05: Merging and Conflicts via Terminal

title: Module 05: Merging and Conflicts via Terminal
text:
Prerequisites

Module 04 — Branching
Your ~/rehab-research/ repository has at least one feature branch



Fast-Forward vs Three-Way Merge
Git chooses the merge strategy automatically based on the history.
Fast-forward merge: The feature branch is ahead of main with no divergence. Git just moves the main pointer forward — no merge commit needed.

%%{init: {'gitGraph': {'theme': 'dark'}} }%%
gitGraph
   commit id: "Initial"
   commit id: "Add normalize"
   branch feature/grip
   commit id: "Add grip fn"
   commit id: "Add tests"
   checkout main
   merge feature/grip id: "Fast-forward"










Three-way merge: Both branches have commits the other does not. Git finds the common ancestor and creates a new merge commit.

%%{init: {'gitGraph': {'theme': 'dark'}} }%%
gitGraph
   commit id: "Initial"
   commit id: "Add normalize"
   branch feature/grip
   commit id: "Add grip fn"
   checkout main
   commit id: "Fix protocol"
   merge feature/grip id: "Merge commit"













git merge: Combining Branches
To merge a feature branch into main:
git switch main                             # be on the branch you are merging INTO
git merge feature/add-grip-strength-measure
Fast-forward output:
Updating 1a5f3b2..3c4d5e6
Fast-forward
 scripts/analysis.R | 4 ++++
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+)
Three-way merge output:
Merge made by the 'ort' strategy.
 scripts/analysis.R | 4 ++++
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+)
A merge commit is created automatically. Git opens your configured editor for the merge commit message — press Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X in nano to save the default message.



Understanding Merge Conflicts
A conflict occurs when both branches modified the same lines of the same file. Git cannot choose automatically — it needs your decision.
When does it happen: - Branch A changes line 5 of analysis.R - Branch B also changes line 5 of analysis.R - Git merges them → conflict on line 5
During a conflict, git status shows:
git status
On branch main
You have unmerged paths.
  (fix conflicts and run "git merge --continue")
  (use "git merge --abort" to abort the merge)

Unmerged paths:
  (use "git add <file>..." to mark resolution)
        both modified:   scripts/analysis.R



Conflict Markers Explained
Open the conflicting file with cat:
cat scripts/analysis.R
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

<<<<<<< HEAD
normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
=======
normalize_balance <- function(x) scale(x, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
>>>>>>> feature/alternative-normalization

filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))
Reading the markers:







Marker
Meaning




<<<<<<< HEAD
Start of YOUR version (current branch)


=======
Divider between the two versions


>>>>>>> branch-name
End of THEIR version (branch being merged in)



Everything between <<<<<<< and ======= is your version. Everything between ======= and >>>>>>> is the incoming version.



Resolving Conflicts with nano

Open the file in nano:
nano scripts/analysis.R
Navigate to the conflict markers using arrow keys.
Delete the three marker lines AND one of the two versions, keeping only the content you want. For example, to keep the incoming version:
Before:
<<<<<<< HEAD
normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
=======
normalize_balance <- function(x) scale(x, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
>>>>>>> feature/alternative-normalization
After (keeping the incoming version):
normalize_balance <- function(x) scale(x, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
Save and exit: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X
Verify the file looks correct:
cat scripts/analysis.R
No <<<<<<<, =======, or >>>>>>> should remain.




Completing the Merge
After resolving all conflicts:
git add scripts/analysis.R     # mark the file as resolved
git status                     # should show "All conflicts fixed but you are still merging"
git merge --continue           # complete the merge (opens editor for merge commit message)
Or in one step after staging all resolved files:
git commit                     # equivalent to merge --continue when in a merge state
Verify:
git log --oneline --graph --all
*   a1b2c3d (HEAD -> main) Merge branch 'feature/alternative-normalization'
|\
| * 8e9f0a1 (feature/alternative-normalization) Try scale() for normalization
* | 3c4d5e6 Fix: correct normalize baseline reference
|/
* 1a5f3b2 Add: normalize_balance function



Aborting a Merge
If you want to undo the merge attempt and return to the pre-merge state:
git merge --abort
(no output — silence means success)
git status
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
The working directory is restored exactly as it was before the merge started. Use --abort any time you want to reconsider your approach before completing.

title: Module 03: Remote Repositories via Terminal
text: In Module 02 you learned to commit locally. Now you will connect to GitHub and push your work to a remote repository — creating a backup, enabling collaboration, and making your research reproducible across machines.

title: Module 03: Remote Repositories via Terminal
text:
Prerequisites

Module 02 — Basic Git Workflow
SSH keys generated and added to GitHub (see your setup guide)
Read the Data Safety section at the end of Module 02 before any exercises here



git clone: Copying a Remote Repository
git clone: downloads an entire repository (all commits, all branches) to your machine.
git clone git@github.com:username/repository-name.git
Cloning into 'repository-name'...
remote: Enumerating objects: 47, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (47/47), done.
remote: Compressing objects: 100% (32/32), done.
Receiving objects: 100% (47/47), 12.54 KiB | 4.18 MiB/s, done.
Resolving deltas: 100% (8/8), done.
After cloning, cd into the new directory:
cd repository-name
git log --oneline
Clone creates a remote named origin pointing back to the source automatically.



Understanding Remotes: git remote -v
git remote -v: lists all configured remote connections for this repository.
git remote -v
origin  git@github.com:username/rehab-research.git (fetch)
origin  git@github.com:username/rehab-research.git (push)

origin — the conventional name for the primary remote (set by git clone automatically)
Two lines per remote: one for fetching (downloading), one for pushing (uploading)

To add a remote manually (when you used git init locally instead of cloning):
git remote add origin git@github.com:username/rehab-research.git



git push: Uploading Commits
git push: sends your local commits to the remote repository.
git push origin main
Enumerating objects: 8, done.
Counting objects: 100% (8/8), done.
Delta compression using up to 8 threads
Compressing objects: 100% (5/5), done.
Writing objects: 100% (6/6), 712 bytes | 712.00 KiB/s, done.
Total 6 (delta 1), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To git@github.com:username/rehab-research.git
   3f2a1b4..1a5f3b2  main -> main
Reading the output:

3f2a1b4..1a5f3b2 — the range of commits pushed (from → to)
main -> main — local branch main pushed to remote branch main

Use -u on the first push to set the upstream tracking branch:
git push -u origin main   # first push
git push                  # all subsequent pushes (upstream is remembered)



git pull: Downloading Changes
git pull: fetches remote changes and merges them into your current branch.
git pull origin main
remote: Enumerating objects: 5, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (5/5), done.
remote: Compressing objects: 100% (2/2), done.
remote: Total 3 (delta 1), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
Unpacking objects: 100% (3/3), done.
From github.com:username/rehab-research
   1a5f3b2..9d4c7e1  main       -> origin/main
Updating 1a5f3b2..9d4c7e1
Fast-forward
 docs/protocol.md | 5 +++++
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)



Fetch vs Pull
git pull is actually two commands in one:

%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    R["Remote\n(GitHub)"]
    L["Local\nrepository"]
    W["Working\ndirectory"]

    R -->|"git fetch\n(download only)"| L
    L -->|"git merge"| W
    R -->|"git pull\n(fetch + merge)"| W










git fetch: downloads changes from the remote but does NOT merge them. Lets you inspect changes before integrating them.
git fetch origin
git log --oneline origin/main   # see what's on the remote
git diff main origin/main        # compare your branch to the remote
git merge origin/main            # merge when ready
Rule of thumb: Use git pull when you trust the remote. Use git fetch + inspect + git merge when working on a branch that others are also modifying.



Authentication: SSH Keys
SSH keys are the recommended authentication method for terminal Git users. They authenticate you automatically without typing a password each time.






Tip



If you completed your platform setup guide, you already have SSH keys. Skip ahead to the verification step.


Generate an SSH key (if you have not already):
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@institution.edu"
Accept the default location. Set a passphrase (recommended).
Copy your public key:

macOS / LinuxWindows (Git Bash)Windows (WSL2)


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub



Add the output to GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key.
Verify SSH works:
ssh -T git@github.com
Hi username! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.



Troubleshooting Remote Operations

Rejected Push (non-fast-forward)
! [rejected]        main -> main (non-fast-forward)
error: failed to push some refs to 'git@github.com:username/repo.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not
hint: have locally. Integrate the remote changes before pushing again.
What it means: The remote has commits you do not have locally. Someone else pushed while you were working.
Fix:
git pull --rebase origin main   # fetch remote commits, replay yours on top
git push origin main             # now push should succeed


Authentication Failure
git@github.com: Permission denied (publickey).
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.
What it means: GitHub does not recognise your SSH key.
Fix steps:
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519     # add key to the agent
ssh -T git@github.com         # verify connection works
git push origin main          # retry
If still failing: confirm the public key in ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub matches what is registered in GitHub Settings → SSH keys.

title: Module 01: Git Conceptual Foundation
text:
Most Git confusion comes from using commands without understanding what Git is doing. This module builds the mental model first — so that when you run git add or git commit, you know exactly what is happening inside your repository.
We will explore the .git directory directly using the terminal commands from Module 00.

title: Module 01: Git Conceptual Foundation
text:
Prerequisites

Module 00 — Terminal Fundamentals

You should be able to cd, ls, and cat files before continuing.


What Is Version Control?
Version control: a system that records changes to files over time so that you can recall specific versions later.
Without version control, researchers track versions manually:
analysis.R
analysis_v2.R
analysis_v2_FINAL.R
analysis_v2_FINAL_corrected.R
analysis_v2_FINAL_corrected_USE_THIS_ONE.R
This is error-prone, takes disk space for every copy, and makes it impossible to understand what changed between versions or why.
Git solves this by recording a complete history of your project — who changed what, when, and with a message explaining why — in a hidden folder called .git.



Git’s Mental Model: Snapshots, Not Diffs
Most version control systems store diffs — the line-by-line changes between versions. Git is different: it stores snapshots of your entire project at each point in time.

%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    subgraph Diff-based
        D1[Version 1] -->|"+ line 5\n- line 12"| D2[Version 2]
        D2 -->|"+ lines 3-4"| D3[Version 3]
    end
    subgraph Snapshot-based Git
        S1[Snapshot 1\nall files] --> S2[Snapshot 2\nall files]
        S2 --> S3[Snapshot 3\nall files]
    end










When a file has not changed between snapshots, Git stores only a pointer to the previous version of that file — it does not duplicate it. This makes Git both efficient and conceptually clean: every commit is a complete picture of your project.



The Three States
Every file in a Git repository is in one of three states:

flowchart LR
    W["Working Directory<br/>(your files on disk)"]
    S["Staging Area<br/>(the index)"]
    R["Repository<br/>(.git/objects)"]

    W -->|"git add"| S
    S -->|"git commit"| R
    R -->|"git checkout / git restore"| W











Working directory: your files as they exist on disk — what you see in Finder/File Explorer
Staging area (also called the index): a preparation area where you assemble the next snapshot. You choose exactly which changes to include.
Repository: the .git directory — the permanent record of every snapshot you have committed

Understanding these three states explains why git add exists. You do not have to commit everything you changed — you can carefully select what goes into each snapshot.



Exploring the .git Directory
The .git directory is where Git stores everything. Let us create a repository and look inside.
cd ~/rehab-research
git init
Initialized empty Git repository in /Users/yourname/rehab-research/.git/
Now list the .git directory:
ls -la .git/
total 24
drwxr-xr-x   9 yourname  staff  288 Feb 28 09:15 .
drwxr-xr-x   5 yourname  staff  160 Feb 28 09:15 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   23 Feb 28 09:15 HEAD        # your current position
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   92 Feb 28 09:15 config      # repository settings
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   73 Feb 28 09:15 description # ignored except by GitWeb
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff   64 Feb 28 09:15 hooks/      # automation scripts
drwxr-xr-x   3 yourname  staff   96 Feb 28 09:15 info/
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 objects/    # Git's object database
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 refs/       # branches and tags
Each entry has a purpose. The important ones:

objects/ — Git’s Database
ls .git/objects/
info  pack
Right now it is almost empty — you have not committed anything yet. After a commit, Git stores blobs (file contents), trees (directory listings), and commits (metadata + pointer to a tree) here. Each is identified by a SHA-1 hash.


refs/ — Branches and Tags
ls .git/refs/
heads  tags
ls .git/refs/heads/
(empty — no branches have a commit yet)
After your first commit, refs/heads/main will appear as a file containing the hash of the latest commit. That is all a branch is: a file containing a hash.


HEAD — Your Current Position
cat .git/HEAD
ref: refs/heads/main
HEAD is a pointer to where you currently are in the repository. Right now it points at refs/heads/main (which does not exist yet because there are no commits). After commits, it will point to the latest commit on your current branch.


config — Repository Configuration
cat .git/config
[core]
    repositoryformatversion = 0
    filemode = true
    bare = false
    logallrefupdates = true
This is your repository’s local configuration — it overrides your global ~/.gitconfig for this repository only.


index — The Staging Area
The index file does not exist until you stage something (git add). It is a binary file that records the exact state of what you are about to commit.




Commits as Snapshots with Metadata
Let us create a file and make a commit so we can inspect the resulting objects:
echo "library(dplyr)" > scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add initial data cleaning script"
[main (root-commit) 3f2a1b4] Add initial data cleaning script
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
 create mode 100644 scripts/analysis.R
The string 3f2a1b4 is the beginning of your commit hash — a 40-character SHA-1 fingerprint of the entire snapshot. Now look at the objects directory:
ls .git/objects/
3f  a8  pack  info
git cat-file -t 3f2a1b4
commit
git cat-file -p 3f2a1b4
tree a8c3d5e2f1...
author Your Name <your@institution.edu> 1709114400 -0500
committer Your Name <your@institution.edu> 1709114400 -0500

Add initial data cleaning script
A commit object contains:

tree: pointer to the snapshot (the directory listing at this point)
parent: pointer to the previous commit (none for the first commit)
author / committer: who made the change and when
message: your description of the change




The Commit Graph as a DAG
As you make more commits, Git builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) — a chain of commits where each one points back to its parent(s).

gitGraph
   commit id: "3f2a1b4 Initial script"
   commit id: "9d4c7e1 Add normalization"
   commit id: "1a5f3b2 Fix baseline calc"










“Directed” means each commit knows its parent (arrows point backwards in time). “Acyclic” means there are no loops — history always moves forward.
This graph is the foundation of branching, merging, and rebasing — everything in the later modules builds on it.



Branches as Movable Pointers
Now look at what the branch file contains after your commit:
cat .git/refs/heads/main
3f2a1b4c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a
That is the full commit hash. A branch is nothing more than a file containing a single commit hash. When you make a new commit, Git updates this file to point to the new commit automatically.

%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    C1["commit 3f2a1b4\nInitial script"] --> C2["commit 9d4c7e1\nAdd normalization"]
    C2 --> C3["commit 1a5f3b2\nFix baseline calc"]
    MAIN["main"] --> C3
    HEAD["HEAD"] --> MAIN

    style MAIN fill:#50fa7b,color:#000
    style HEAD fill:#bd93f9,color:#000










When you create a new branch, Git creates a new file in refs/heads/ pointing to the same commit. Branches are cheap because they are just tiny files.



HEAD and Detached HEAD
HEAD normally points to a branch name (e.g., refs/heads/main), which in turn points to a commit. This is the attached state.
cat .git/HEAD
ref: refs/heads/main
If you check out a commit directly (by hash or tag), HEAD points to the commit itself — not to a branch. This is called detached HEAD:
# Hypothetical — do not run this yet
git checkout 3f2a1b4
cat .git/HEAD
3f2a1b4c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a
In detached HEAD, any commits you make will not belong to any branch and can be lost. Module 10 covers how to recover from this. For now, just know the concept.

title: Advanced_Git
section: Why Learn Git via the Terminal?
text:
Why Learn Git via the Terminal?
Rehabilitation science increasingly demands reproducible, documented workflows. The terminal gives you that — and more:

Reproducibility: Every command you run can be copied, saved, and shared exactly
Documentation: Your terminal history is your audit trail
Portability: Terminal commands work identically on every machine, every server, every collaborator’s computer
Understanding: The terminal shows you what Git is actually doing — no black boxes

GUI tools hide the mechanics that matter when things go wrong. The terminal puts you in control.

title: Advanced_Git
text:
What You Will Learn
After completing this course, you will be able to:

Navigate file systems and run commands in the terminal
Understand Git’s internal structure (the .git repository)
Explain Git’s conceptual model: snapshots, commits, branches, HEAD
Perform all basic Git operations from the command line
Create and manage branches entirely in the terminal
Resolve merge conflicts using terminal text editors
Clean commit history with interactive rebase
Collaborate via terminal-based workflows
Protect sensitive research data with proper .gitignore configuration
Troubleshoot Git problems by understanding what Git does internally
Document research workflows through commit history

title: Exercises: Module 11 — Git with Research Tools
text:
Complete Module 11 and Exercises 02. These exercises work with your global Git configuration and the .git/hooks/ directory — changes persist across all repositories.



Goal: Configure four aliases that speed up daily Git use, then verify each works as expected.
Steps:

Add all four aliases to your global config:
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --all --decorate"
git config --global alias.st status
git config --global alias.unstage "restore --staged"
git config --global alias.last "log -1 HEAD --stat"
Verify all four are in your config:
cat ~/.gitconfig







Expected Output (alias section)





[alias]
    lg = log --oneline --graph --all --decorate
    st = status
    unstage = restore --staged
    last = log -1 HEAD --stat




Test each alias:
cd ~/rehab-research

# Short status
git st







Expected Output





On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean



# Graph log
git lg






Expected Output





* 3c4d5e6 (HEAD -> main) Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
* 9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
* 3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
Full branch graph in one short command.



# Last commit with stats
git last






Expected Output





commit 3c4d5e6 (HEAD -> main)
Author: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 14:23:11 2026 +0100

    Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index

 scripts/analysis.R | 2 +-
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)



# Unstage — test by staging a file first
echo "# test" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git st






Expected Output





On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R



git unstage scripts/analysis.R
git st






Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
The file was moved back from staging to the working directory. Clean up:
git restore scripts/analysis.R



What you learned:

git config --global alias.NAME "command" creates a permanent shortcut
Aliases are stored in ~/.gitconfig and work in every repository
Aliases can wrap complex multi-flag commands into a single short name





Goal: Write a shell script that automatically prevents accidental commits of files from the data/ directory, and test it with a real staged data file.
Steps:

Open the pre-commit hook file:
cd ~/rehab-research
nano .git/hooks/pre-commit
Write the following script exactly:
#!/bin/bash
# Block commits that stage files in the data/ directory

staged_data=$(git diff --cached --name-only | grep -E "^data/")

if [ -n "$staged_data" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Staged file(s) found in data/ directory:"
    echo "$staged_data"
    echo ""
    echo "Research data must not be committed."
    echo "Steps to fix:"
    echo "  1. git restore --staged <file>"
    echo "  2. Add the pattern to .gitignore"
    exit 1
fi
Save: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X
Make the hook executable:
chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit
Verify the hook file is executable:
ls -la .git/hooks/pre-commit







Expected Output





-rwxr-xr-x  1 jbayer  staff  287 Feb 28 16:30 .git/hooks/pre-commit
The x bits confirm the file is executable for user, group, and others.




Test the hook by staging a data file and attempting a commit:
mkdir -p data
echo "id,grip_kg" > data/test-participants.csv
git add data/test-participants.csv
git commit -m "Test: this commit should be blocked by the hook"







Expected Output





ERROR: Staged file(s) found in data/ directory:
data/test-participants.csv

Research data must not be committed.
Steps to fix:
  1. git restore --staged <file>
  2. Add the pattern to .gitignore
The commit is blocked. The hook exited with code 1 which causes git commit to abort.




Verify the commit did NOT happen:
git log --oneline -3
git status







Expected Output





3c4d5e6 (HEAD -> main) Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
...

On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        new file:   data/test-participants.csv
No new commit in git log. The file is still staged — the hook blocked the commit without removing the staging.




Fix the situation and clean up:
git restore --staged data/test-participants.csv
echo 'data/' >> .gitignore
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Data: add .gitignore to protect data directory"
Confirm the hook still works with a safe commit:
echo "# Safe comment" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Docs: add safe comment to analysis script"







Expected Output





[main 7e8f9a0] Docs: add safe comment to analysis script
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
The hook runs, finds no data/ files staged, and allows the commit through.



What you learned:

Pre-commit hooks run as shell scripts before every commit
Exiting with code 1 (exit 1) aborts the commit
git diff --cached --name-only lists staged files — the hook reads this list
Hooks must be executable (chmod +x) to run







Common Mistakes




Forgetting chmod +x: The hook file exists but Git silently ignores non-executable hooks. Always verify with ls -la .git/hooks/pre-commit.
Skipping hooks in an emergency: git commit --no-verify bypasses all hooks. This exists for genuine emergencies — do not use it as a workaround for the hook catching a real problem.
Hooks are not shared: The file is inside .git/ which is not committed. Every collaborator must install the hook manually on their machine.

Forgetting chmod +x: The hook file exists but Git silently ignores non-executable hooks. Always verify with ls -la .git/hooks/pre-commit.
Skipping hooks in an emergency: git commit --no-verify bypasses all hooks. This exists for genuine emergencies — do not use it as a workaround for the hook catching a real problem.
Hooks are not shared: The file is inside .git/ which is not committed. Every collaborator must install the hook manually on their machine.

title: Exercises: Module 09 — Collaboration
text:
Complete Module 09 and Exercises 03.
Requirements: - gh auth status returns a logged-in status - SSH authentication is working (ssh -T git@github.com succeeds) - A GitHub account with a repository to fork, or ask your instructor for the practice repository URL



Goal: Complete the full forking workflow: fork a repository, create a feature branch, make a research-relevant change, and open a pull request from the terminal.
Setup: For this exercise you need a GitHub repository to fork. Use one of: - A repository provided by your instructor - Your own rehab-research repository (fork yourself to practise the workflow) - Any public repository you have permission to contribute to
Replace OWNER/REPO with the actual repository path throughout.
Steps:

Fork and clone the repository:
cd ~
gh repo fork OWNER/REPO --clone
cd REPO







Expected Output





✓ Created fork yourusername/REPO
Cloning into 'REPO'...
...
✓ Cloned fork
Your fork is at github.com/yourusername/REPO. Your local clone has both origin (your fork) and upstream (the original) configured automatically.




Verify both remotes are configured:
git remote -v







Expected Output





origin      git@github.com:yourusername/REPO.git (fetch)
origin      git@github.com:yourusername/REPO.git (push)
upstream    git@github.com:OWNER/REPO.git (fetch)
upstream    git@github.com:OWNER/REPO.git (push)




Create a feature branch:
git switch -c feature/add-baseline-measures
Make a meaningful change — add a comment or small function to a script:
# If the repository has an R script:
echo "" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "# Baseline measure: grip strength asymmetry index" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "grip_asymmetry_index <- function(dominant, non_dominant) {" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "  abs(dominant - non_dominant) / ((dominant + non_dominant) / 2)" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "}" >> scripts/analysis.R
Adjust the file path to match the repository structure.
Commit with a research-quality message:
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: grip_asymmetry_index function for bilateral strength comparison"
Push to your fork:
git push origin feature/add-baseline-measures







Expected Output





Enumerating objects: 5, done.
...
To git@github.com:yourusername/REPO.git
 * [new branch]      feature/add-baseline-measures -> feature/add-baseline-measures




Open a pull request:
gh pr create \
  --title "Add: grip_asymmetry_index for bilateral strength comparison" \
  --body "Adds a function to compute the grip asymmetry index (|D - ND| / mean).

This measure is used in:
- Smith et al. (2024) Table 3
- The Wave 2 analysis protocol (docs/protocol.md)

**Testing**: Function verified against published reference values.
**No data files staged** — confirmed with git status."







Expected Output





Creating pull request for yourusername:feature/add-baseline-measures into main in OWNER/REPO

https://github.com/OWNER/REPO/pull/XX
The URL links to your PR on GitHub.




Verify the PR exists:
gh pr list







Expected Output





Showing 1 of 1 open pull request in OWNER/REPO

#XX  Add: grip_asymmetry_index for bilateral strength comparison  feature/add-baseline-measures  about 1 minute ago



What you learned:

gh repo fork --clone forks and clones in one step with both remotes configured
Feature branches on forks follow the same naming conventions as local branches
gh pr create opens a pull request with a title and body from the terminal





Goal: Simulate a situation where the upstream repository has received new commits while you were working on your fork, and synchronize correctly.
Setup: To simulate upstream activity, open a second terminal or ask a collaborator to push a commit to the upstream repository. If working alone:

Temporarily add a commit directly to the upstream (if you own it):
# In the UPSTREAM repository (not your fork)
# Add a small documentation change to simulate upstream activity
echo "# Updated by upstream maintainer" >> README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Docs: update README with Wave 2 contact information"
git push origin main
Then return to your fork’s local directory:
cd ~/REPO

Steps:

Fetch changes from upstream (does not affect your local branches):
git fetch upstream







Expected Output





remote: Enumerating objects: 5, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (5/5), done.
Receiving objects: 100% (3/3), 412 bytes | 412.00 KiB/s, done.
From git@github.com:OWNER/REPO
   3c4d5e6..7e8f9a0  main -> upstream/main
The 3c4d5e6..7e8f9a0 shows upstream/main advanced from one hash to another. Your local main is not yet updated.




See what new commits exist on upstream:
git log --oneline upstream/main







Expected Output





7e8f9a0 Docs: update README with Wave 2 contact information
3c4d5e6 Initial commit
...
The new commit at the top is what upstream added since you forked.




Rebase your local main onto upstream/main:
git switch main
git rebase upstream/main







Expected Output





Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/main.
Your local main now includes upstream’s new commits with a linear history.




Push the updated main to your fork:
git push origin main







Expected Output





Total 0 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To git@github.com:yourusername/REPO.git
   3c4d5e6..7e8f9a0  main -> main
Your fork’s main on GitHub now matches upstream.




Verify the full history:
git log --oneline --graph --all







Expected Output





* c4d5e6f (HEAD -> feature/add-baseline-measures) Add: grip_asymmetry_index for bilateral strength comparison
* 7e8f9a0 (upstream/main, origin/main, main) Docs: update README with Wave 2 contact information
* 3c4d5e6 Initial commit
Your feature branch is one commit ahead of the synchronized main.



What you learned:

git fetch upstream downloads upstream changes without modifying local branches
git log upstream/main shows upstream’s history before you incorporate it
git rebase upstream/main replays your local main onto the upstream tip
This workflow keeps your fork current without merge commits







Common Mistakes




Using git pull upstream main instead of fetch + rebase: git pull by default merges, creating a merge commit in your main. Use git pull --rebase upstream main or the separate fetch + rebase steps to keep history linear.
Rebasing a branch others have pulled: If a collaborator has pulled from your fork’s main, rebasing rewrites the hashes they have locally. Coordinate before rebasing shared branches.
