title: Exercises: Module 07 — Advanced Operations
text:
Complete Module 07 and Exercises 06. You should have ~/rehab-research/ with several commits in history.



Goal: Simulate a real research scenario — your balance scores changed unexpectedly — and use git log -S to find the exact commit where the normalization formula was modified.
Setup: Ensure you have commits that added and modified the normalize_balance function. If you followed earlier exercises, you already do. If not, create them now:
cd ~/rehab-research
git switch main

# Commit 1: add the function
echo "normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: normalize_balance with min-max scaling"

# Commit 2: change the function
sed -i.bak 's/normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) \/ (max(x) - min(x)) \* 100/normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) \/ sd(x)/' scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Fix: switch to z-score normalization per Smith et al. (2024)"
Steps:

Search for all commits that added or removed normalize_balance:
git log -S "normalize_balance" --oneline







Expected Output





3c4d5e6 Fix: switch to z-score normalization per Smith et al. (2024)
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance with min-max scaling
Two commits appear — the original addition and the modification.




Inspect the most recent change to see exactly what changed:
git show 3c4d5e6







Expected Output





commit 3c4d5e6...
Author: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   ...

    Fix: switch to z-score normalization per Smith et al. (2024)

diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index a1b2c3d..4e5f6a7 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -4,4 +4,4 @@ library(tidyr)
-normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
+normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
The - line shows what was removed, the + line shows what replaced it. You can now trace exactly when and why the formula changed.




Search by commit message pattern to find all “Fix” commits:
git log --grep "Fix:" --oneline







Expected Output





3c4d5e6 Fix: switch to z-score normalization per Smith et al. (2024)
1a5f3b2 Fix: correct normalization formula
All commits whose messages begin with “Fix:” are listed. Useful for extracting an audit trail of all corrections made to your analysis.




Use git blame to see who last modified each line of analysis.R:
git blame scripts/analysis.R







Expected Output





9d4c7e1a (Dr. J. Bayer  2026-02-22  1) library(dplyr)
9d4c7e1a (Dr. J. Bayer  2026-02-22  2) library(tidyr)
9d4c7e1a (Dr. J. Bayer  2026-02-22  3)
3c4d5e6f (Dr. J. Bayer  2026-02-28  4) normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
Each line shows: hash, author, date, line number, and content. Line 4 (the normalize function) was last modified in commit 3c4d5e6f.



What you learned:

git log -S "string" finds commits where a specific string was added or removed
git show HASH reveals the complete diff of any commit
git blame FILE shows line-by-line attribution — who changed what and when
Combine these tools to answer “when did this change and why?” in seconds





Goal: Simulate an interruption mid-analysis — an urgent fix on another branch — and use git stash to preserve your work without committing.
Steps:

Start editing analysis.R without finishing or committing:
cd ~/rehab-research
git switch main
echo "# TODO: add grip asymmetry index calculation" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "grip_asymmetry <- function(d, nd) abs(d - nd) / ((d + nd) / 2)" >> scripts/analysis.R
Check status — you have uncommitted changes:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R




An urgent fix is needed on fix/normalize-balance-scores. Stash your current work:
git stash save "WIP: grip asymmetry index — not ready to commit"







Expected Output





Saved working directory and index state On main: WIP: grip asymmetry index — not ready to commit
Your working directory is now clean.




Verify the working directory is clean and your changes are saved:
git status
git stash list







Expected Output





On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean

stash@{0}: On main: WIP: grip asymmetry index — not ready to commit




Switch to the fix branch, make an urgent correction, and commit:
git switch -c fix/normalize-balance-scores
# Simulate an urgent fix
echo "# Corrected sd() usage" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Fix: ensure sd() not var() used in normalize_balance"
Switch back to main and restore your stashed work:
git switch main
git stash pop







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R

Dropped stash@{0} (7f8a9b0c1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a)
Your work-in-progress edits are restored. The stash is removed from the list.




Verify your changes are back:
tail -3 scripts/analysis.R







Expected Output





# TODO: add grip asymmetry index calculation
grip_asymmetry <- function(d, nd) abs(d - nd) / ((d + nd) / 2)
Your asymmetry index work is back exactly as you left it.



What you learned:

git stash save "message" saves uncommitted work to a stack
git stash list shows all saved stashes
git stash pop restores the most recent stash and removes it from the stack
Stash lets you switch branches without committing incomplete work







Common Mistakes




Popping the wrong stash: git stash pop always pops stash@{0} (most recent). Use git stash apply stash@{1} to apply a specific stash by index.
Forgetting about old stashes: Run git stash list regularly. Old stashes are not pushed to remotes and will be lost if you delete the repository.

title: Exercises: Module 05 — Merging and Conflicts
text:
Complete Module 05 and Exercises 04. You should have at least two branches in ~/rehab-research/.



Goal: Deliberately create a conflict between two branches, then resolve it using nano.
Setup:

Make sure you are on main:
cd ~/rehab-research
git switch main
Modify line 3 of analysis.R on main:
# View current content
cat -n scripts/analysis.R
Edit with nano and change the normalize_balance function body:
nano scripts/analysis.R
Change the normalize function to:
normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
Save: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index"
Switch to feature/add-grip-strength-measure and make a DIFFERENT change to the same line:
git switch feature/add-grip-strength-measure
nano scripts/analysis.R
Change the normalize function to:
normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
Save and commit:
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Fix: use min-max normalization (0-100 range) for balance"

Now create the conflict:

Switch to main and attempt to merge:
git switch main

Auto-merging scripts/analysis.R

Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.




Check the conflict status:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
You have unmerged paths.
  (fix conflicts and run "git merge --continue")
  (use "git merge --abort" to abort the merge)

Unmerged paths:
  (use "git add <file>..." to mark resolution)
        both modified:   scripts/analysis.R




View the conflict markers:
cat scripts/analysis.R







Expected Output





library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

<<<<<<< HEAD
normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
=======
normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
>>>>>>> feature/add-grip-strength-measure

measure_grip_strength <- function(hand, trials) mean(trials[hand])
HEAD version = z-score (your main branch). Feature version = min-max (incoming branch).




Resolve the conflict using nano:
nano scripts/analysis.R
Delete the three marker lines and the z-score version, keeping the min-max version:
normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
Save and exit.
Verify no conflict markers remain:
cat scripts/analysis.R
Confirm: no <<<<<<<, =======, or >>>>>>> in the file.

git status







Expected Output





On branch main
All conflicts fixed but you are still merging.
  (use "git merge --continue" to continue)
  (use "git merge --abort" to abort the merge)

Changes to be committed:
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R



git merge --continue
Nano opens for the merge commit message — save the default and exit.

Verify the merge:
git log --oneline --graph --all







Expected Output





*   a7b8c9d (HEAD -> main) Merge branch 'feature/add-grip-strength-measure'
|\
| * 3c4d5e6 Fix: use min-max normalization for balance
* | 9f0a1b2 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
|/
* 1a5f3b2 Add: filter_complete_cases to exclude missing values
The diamond shape shows the divergence and merge. Both lines of work combined.



What you learned:

Conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>) show both versions

git merge --continue completes the merge







Common Mistakes




Left conflict markers in the file: git add will succeed, but the file will be broken. Always cat the file to verify before staging.
Used git commit instead of git merge --continue: Both work when in a merge state, but git merge --continue is more explicit about your intent.







Goal: Practice aborting a merge and returning to a clean state.
Setup: Create another conflict situation.
Steps:

Create a new branch and modify analysis.R:
git switch -c fix/alternative-filter
echo "" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "filter_by_age <- function(df, min_age) dplyr::filter(df, age >= min_age)" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: filter_by_age function for age-based inclusion criteria"
Modify the same lines on main:
git switch main
echo "" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "filter_by_age <- function(df, max_age) dplyr::filter(df, age <= max_age)" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: filter_by_age with upper age limit for exclusion"
Attempt the merge:
git merge fix/alternative-filter







Expected Output





Auto-merging scripts/analysis.R
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in scripts/analysis.R
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.




Decide you are not ready — abort:
git merge --abort
Verify the working directory is clean:
git status
cat scripts/analysis.R

nothing to commit, working tree clean
The file shows your main version with no conflict markers. The merge never happened — you are back where you started.



What you learned:

git merge --abort completely reverses the merge attempt
You can safely abort at any point before git merge --continue
Aborting does not affect any commits — only the in-progress merge

title: Exercises: Module 03 — Remote Repositories
text:
Complete:

Module 03 reading
Exercises 02 — your local repository has at least 3 commits
SSH key generated and added to GitHub (see your setup guide)







Data Safety Reminder



Before pushing anything: ensure your .gitignore excludes data/, .env, and any files with participant data. Run git status and check every file listed.





Goal: Confirm SSH authentication is working between your terminal and GitHub.
Steps:

Check if an SSH key exists:
ls ~/.ssh/







Expected Output (if key exists)





id_ed25519      id_ed25519.pub      known_hosts
If you only see known_hosts or the directory is empty, generate a key first.




If no key exists, generate one:
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@institution.edu"
Press Return to accept defaults. Set a passphrase when prompted.
Display your public key:
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub







Expected Output





ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAI... your@institution.edu
Copy this entire line. Add it to GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key.




Test the connection:
ssh -T git@github.com







Expected Output (success)





Hi username! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
If you see this, SSH is working. The “no shell access” part is expected and normal.









Tip



If you get “Permission denied (publickey)”: Run ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 to load your key into the SSH agent, then try again. If still failing, confirm the key in GitHub Settings matches the output of cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub.






Goal: Create a remote repository and push your local commits to it.
Setup: Create an empty repository on GitHub first: 1. Go to github.com → + → New repository 2. Name: rehab-research 3. Leave it completely empty (no README, no .gitignore, no license) 4. Copy the SSH URL: git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
Steps:

Navigate to your local repository:
cd ~/rehab-research
git status
Add the remote:
git remote add origin git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
git remote -v







Expected Output





origin  git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git (fetch)
origin  git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git (push)




Push your commits:
git push -u origin main







Expected Output





Enumerating objects: 12, done.
Counting objects: 100% (12/12), done.
Delta compression using up to 8 threads
Compressing objects: 100% (8/8), done.
Writing objects: 100% (12/12), 1.02 KiB | 1.02 MiB/s, done.
Total 12 (delta 2), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
 * [new branch]      main -> main
Branch 'main' set up to track remote branch 'main' from 'origin'.
[new branch] confirms the remote branch was created. “set up to track” means future git push and git pull will use this remote automatically.




Verify the push worked:
git log --oneline origin/main







Expected Output





b3a2c1d (HEAD -> main, origin/main) Add: .gitignore to exclude data files
1a5f3b2 Add: filter_complete_cases to exclude missing grip strength values
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
origin/main now appears in the log alongside main — they are in sync.







Goal: Deliberately create a diverged history, encounter the “rejected” error, and fix it.
Context: This simulates what happens when a collaborator pushes while you are working.
Steps:

Make a local commit:
echo "# Wave 2 analysis placeholder" >> docs/protocol.md
git add docs/protocol.md
git commit -m "Docs: add Wave 2 placeholder to protocol"
Use git commit --amend to change the commit (this rewrites its hash, creating divergence):
git commit --amend -m "Docs: Wave 2 data collection protocol placeholder"
Attempt to push:
git push origin main







Expected Output (the rejected error)





 ! [rejected]        main -> main (non-fast-forward)
error: failed to push some refs to 'git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not
hint: have locally. Integrate the remote changes (e.g.
hint: 'git pull --rebase ...') before pushing again.
Git is telling you: the remote has commits your local branch does not have. The hint even tells you the fix: git pull --rebase.




Fix it with rebase-pull:
git pull --rebase origin main







Expected Output





Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/main.
Or if there is a conflict, it will show the conflicting files. For this exercise there should be none.




Push again:
git push origin main







Expected Output





Enumerating objects: 5, done.
...
To git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
   b3a2c1d..c4d5e6f  main -> main
Success. The push went through.



What you learned:

Rejected push = remote has commits you do not have locally
git pull --rebase origin main is the standard fix
--rebase replays your commits on top of the fetched commits (cleaner than merge)







Common Mistakes




Using git push --force: This overwrites the remote history and can delete collaborators’ work. Never use --force on a shared branch. Use --rebase instead.
git pull without --rebase: Creates an unnecessary merge commit. Prefer --rebase for keeping history clean.

title: Exercises: Module 01 — Git Conceptual Foundation
text:
Make sure you have completed:

Module 00 — Terminal Fundamentals
Module 01 — Git Conceptual Foundation
Exercise 00.3: Your ~/rehab-research/ folder exists




Goal: Use git init to create a repository and inspect every file in the .git directory.
Steps:

Navigate to your project folder:
cd ~/rehab-research
Initialize a Git repository:
git init







Expected Output





Initialized empty Git repository in /Users/yourname/rehab-research/.git/
Git created the .git directory. Your project files are unchanged.




List the .git directory contents in detail:
ls -la .git/







Expected Output





total 24
drwxr-xr-x   9 yourname  staff  288 Feb 28 09:15 .
drwxr-xr-x   5 yourname  staff  160 Feb 28 09:15 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   23 Feb 28 09:15 HEAD
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   92 Feb 28 09:15 config
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   73 Feb 28 09:15 description
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff   64 Feb 28 09:15 hooks/
drwxr-xr-x   3 yourname  staff   96 Feb 28 09:15 info/
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 objects/
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 refs/
You should see: HEAD, config, description, hooks/, info/, objects/, refs/.




Look inside objects/ and refs/:
ls .git/objects/
ls .git/refs/
ls .git/refs/heads/







Expected Output





info  pack
heads  tags
(empty)
objects/ only has info/ and pack/ — no commits yet. refs/heads/ is empty — no branches with commits yet.



What you learned:

git init creates the .git directory that holds all of Git’s data
The objects/ directory is Git’s database — empty until you commit
refs/heads/ holds branch files — empty until you make a commit







Common Mistakes




.git does not appear in ls -la: Hidden directories start with . and require the -a flag. Always use ls -la .git/ not ls .git/.
git init says “Reinitialized existing Git repository”: You ran git init in a directory that already had a .git directory. This is harmless — Git just confirms it is already set up.







Goal: Use cat to read HEAD and config, then make a commit and inspect what changed.
Steps:

Read the HEAD file:
cat .git/HEAD







Expected Output





ref: refs/heads/main
HEAD points to the main branch. The branch does not exist yet (no commits), but HEAD is already prepared to point there.




Read the config file:
cat .git/config







Expected Output





[core]
    repositoryformatversion = 0
    filemode = true
    bare = false
    logallrefupdates = true
This is the repository’s local configuration. When you run git config --local, you are editing this file.




Make your first commit (so we can inspect the resulting objects):
echo "library(dplyr)" > scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add initial data cleaning script"







Expected Output





[main (root-commit) 3f2a1b4] Add initial data cleaning script
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
 create mode 100644 scripts/analysis.R
The hash (3f2a1b4) will be different on your machine — it is computed from the commit’s exact contents, author, timestamp, and parent.




Now look at what refs/heads/main contains:
cat .git/refs/heads/main







Expected Output





3f2a1b4c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a
Your hash will be different. This is the full commit hash that main currently points to. A branch is literally just this: a file containing a 40-character hash.




List the objects/ directory again:
ls .git/objects/







Expected Output





1a  3f  a8  info  pack
New two-character subdirectories appeared — these are the first two characters of each object’s hash. Git stores objects this way to avoid having too many files in a single directory.



What you learned:

HEAD always points to your current position (usually a branch)
A branch is a file containing a commit hash — nothing more
After a commit, objects/ grows with new blob, tree, and commit objects





Goal: Create a file, stage it, and interpret every line of git status output.
Steps:

Create a new file:
echo "# Grip Strength Study Protocol" > docs/protocol.md
Run git status and read the output carefully:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        docs/protocol.md

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
Reading this output:

On branch main — you are on the main branch
Untracked files — Git sees the file but is not tracking it yet
docs/protocol.md — this specific file needs to be staged before Git will include it
The hint use "git add <file>..." tells you exactly what to do next





Stage the file:
git add docs/protocol.md
Run git status again:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        new file:   docs/protocol.md
What changed:

The file moved from “Untracked” to “Changes to be committed”
new file: indicates this is being tracked for the first time
The hint now shows git restore --staged to undo the staging




What you learned:

git status always tells you the current state — run it constantly
“Untracked” means Git sees the file but is ignoring it
“Changes to be committed” means the file is staged and ready for a commit
The parenthetical hints in git status are suggestions — read them







Common Mistakes




git status says “not a git repository”: You ran it outside ~/rehab-research/. Run cd ~/rehab-research and try again.
File does not appear as “Untracked”: The file might not exist. Run ls docs/ to check. If it is not there, the echo command may have had a typo.

-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   92 Feb 28 09:15 config
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   73 Feb 28 09:15 description
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff   64 Feb 28 09:15 hooks/
drwxr-xr-x   3 yourname  staff   96 Feb 28 09:15 info/
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 objects/
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 refs/
You should see: HEAD, config, description, hooks/, info/, objects/, refs/.




Look inside objects/ and refs/:
ls .git/objects/
ls .git/refs/
ls .git/refs/heads/







Expected Output





info  pack
heads  tags
(empty)
objects/ only has info/ and pack/ — no commits yet. refs/heads/ is empty — no branches with commits yet.



What you learned:

git init creates the .git directory that holds all of Git’s data
The objects/ directory is Git’s database — empty until you commit
refs/heads/ holds branch files — empty until you make a commit







Common Mistakes




.git does not appear in ls -la: Hidden directories start with . and require the -a flag. Always use ls -la .git/ not ls .git/.
git init says “Reinitialized existing Git repository”: You ran git init in a directory that already had a .git directory. This is harmless — Git just confirms it is already set up.







Goal: Use cat to read HEAD and config, then make a commit and inspect what changed.
Steps:

Read the HEAD file:
cat .git/HEAD







Expected Output





ref: refs/heads/main
HEAD points to the main branch. The branch does not exist yet (no commits), but HEAD is already prepared to point there.




Read the config file:
cat .git/config







Expected Output





[core]
    repositoryformatversion = 0
    filemode = true
    bare = false
    logallrefupdates = true
This is the repository’s local configuration. When you run git config --local, you are editing this file.




Make your first commit (so we can inspect the resulting objects):
echo "library(dplyr)" > scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add initial data cleaning script"







Expected Output





[main (root-commit) 3f2a1b4] Add initial data cleaning script
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
 create mode 100644 scripts/analysis.R
The hash (3f2a1b4) will be different on your machine — it is computed from the commit’s exact contents, author, timestamp, and parent.




Now look at what refs/heads/main contains:
cat .git/refs/heads/main







Expected Output





3f2a1b4c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a
Your hash will be different. This is the full commit hash that main currently points to. A branch is literally just this: a file containing a 40-character hash.




List the objects/ directory again:
ls .git/objects/







Expected Output





1a  3f  a8  info  pack
New two-character subdirectories appeared — these are the first two characters of each object’s hash. Git stores objects this way to avoid having too many files in a single directory.



What you learned:

HEAD always points to your current position (usually a branch)
A branch is a file containing a commit hash — nothing more
After a commit, objects/ grows with new blob, tree, and commit objects





Goal: Create a file, stage it, and interpret every line of git status output.
Steps:

Create a new file:
echo "# Grip Strength Study Protocol" > docs/protocol.md
Run git status and read the output carefully:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        docs/protocol.md

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
Reading this output:

On branch main — you are on the main branch
Untracked files — Git sees the file but is not tracking it yet
docs/protocol.md — this specific file needs to be staged before Git will include it
The hint use "git add <file>..." tells you exactly what to do next





Stage the file:
git add docs/protocol.md
Run git status again:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        new file:   docs/protocol.md
What changed:

The file moved from “Untracked” to “Changes to be committed”
new file: indicates this is being tracked for the first time
The hint now shows git restore --staged to undo the staging




What you learned:

git status always tells you the current state — run it constantly
“Untracked” means Git sees the file but is ignoring it
“Changes to be committed” means the file is staged and ready for a commit
The parenthetical hints in git status are suggestions — read them







Common Mistakes




git status says “not a git repository”: You ran it outside ~/rehab-research/. Run cd ~/rehab-research and try again.
File does not appear as “Untracked”: The file might not exist. Run ls docs/ to check. If it is not there, the echo command may have had a typo.

title: Exercises: Module 01 — Git Conceptual Foundation
text:
Summary
You have completed Module 01 exercises. At this point you can:

Initialize a Git repository with git init
Explore the .git directory and identify its key components
Read HEAD and understand what it points to
Recognize that a branch is a file containing a commit hash
Interpret all three states in git status output

You are ready for Module 02 — Basic Git Workflow.

title: Exercises: Module 00 — Terminal Fundamentals
text:
Make sure you have completed the Module 00 reading and installed a terminal on your operating system.
These exercises build on each other — complete them in order.



Goal: Open a terminal, identify its components, and run your first command.
Steps:

Open a terminal using the method for your operating system (see Module 00 — Opening a Terminal).
Look at your prompt. Identify the username, hostname, and current directory:
# Your prompt will look something like this — do not type this line
yourname@hostname ~ $
Run your first command:
pwd







Expected Output





/Users/yourname
Your output will show your actual username. The path ends with your username because ~ (home directory) expands to /Users/yourname on macOS, /home/yourname on Linux, and /c/Users/yourname on Windows Git Bash.




Run whoami to confirm your username:
whoami







Expected Output





yourname



What you learned:

The terminal prompt shows who you are, which machine you are on, and where you are
pwd prints your current location in the file system
Commands produce output immediately below where you type them







Common Mistakes




Typed the $ symbol: The $ is the prompt — it already exists. Only type what comes after it.
Nothing happened after pressing Return: Check that you pressed Return (not just Enter on the numpad on some keyboards). If nothing happens, press Ctrl + C to cancel and try again.







Goal: Use cd and ls to move through your file system and list directory contents.
Steps:

Go to your home directory:
cd ~
List everything in your home directory, including hidden files:
ls -la







Expected Output





total 64
drwxr-xr-x  20 yourname  staff   640 Feb 28 09:00 .
drwxr-xr-x   8 root      wheel   256 Feb 20 08:00 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   220 Feb 28 09:00 .bash_profile
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff  3771 Feb 28 09:00 .bashrc
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff    64 Feb 28 09:00 Desktop
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff    64 Feb 28 09:00 Documents
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff    64 Feb 28 09:00 Downloads
Your output will differ — you may have different folders. Items starting with . are hidden files (configuration files). The d at the start of drwxr-xr-x means it is a directory; - means it is a file.




Navigate into your Documents folder:
cd Documents
pwd







Expected Output





/Users/yourname/Documents




Go back to your home directory in one step:
cd ~
pwd







Expected Output





/Users/yourname



What you learned:

cd ~ always returns you home, no matter where you are
ls -la reveals hidden files and shows file details
cd .. moves up one level; cd ~ goes all the way home







Common Mistakes




cd Documents says “no such file or directory”: Directory names are case-sensitive on macOS and Linux. Try ls first to see the exact name.
Got lost: Run cd ~ to return home and pwd to confirm where you are.







Goal: Create the rehab-research/ project structure you will use throughout this course.
Steps:

Go to your home directory:
cd ~
Create the project directory with all subdirectories at once:
mkdir -p rehab-research/scripts rehab-research/data rehab-research/docs
Verify the structure was created:
ls rehab-research/







Expected Output





data    docs    scripts
Three directories created. data/ is where your (gitignored) data files will live. scripts/ is where your R and Python analysis scripts go. docs/ is for protocol documents and notes.




Navigate into the scripts directory and create a placeholder file:
cd rehab-research/scripts
touch analysis.R
ls







Expected Output





analysis.R




Confirm your location with pwd:
pwd







Expected Output





/Users/yourname/rehab-research/scripts




Go back to the project root:
cd ~/rehab-research
pwd







Expected Output





/Users/yourname/rehab-research



What you learned:

mkdir -p creates nested directories in a single command
touch creates an empty file
pwd confirms your location at any time
Using ~/ as the start of a path guarantees the path works from anywhere







Common Mistakes




mkdir rehab-research/scripts fails with “No such file or directory”: You forgot the -p flag. Without it, mkdir will not create intermediate directories. Always use mkdir -p for nested paths.
ls shows nothing after mkdir: Check that you are in the right directory with pwd. If pwd shows /Users/yourname/rehab-research, then ls should show data docs scripts.

title: Exercises: Module 02 — Basic Git Workflow
text:
Complete:

Module 02 reading
Exercises 01 — you should have ~/rehab-research/ with a git repository and at least one commit




Goal: Create scripts/analysis.R, make a meaningful first commit, then make two more commits documenting different analytical decisions.
Steps:

Navigate to your project:
cd ~/rehab-research
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
If you see untracked files from Module 01 exercises, you can commit or ignore them.




Create the analysis script with initial content:
echo "library(dplyr)" > scripts/analysis.R
echo "library(tidyr)" >> scripts/analysis.R
cat scripts/analysis.R







Expected Output





library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)




Stage and commit:
git add scripts/analysis.R
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        new file:   scripts/analysis.R



git commit -m "Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr"






Expected Output





[main 3f2a1b4] Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 scripts/analysis.R
Your hash will differ. The message create mode 100644 means this is a new file.




Add a second analytical decision — a normalization function:
echo "" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores"
Add a third decision — a filtering step:
echo "" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: filter_complete_cases to exclude missing grip strength values"
View your history:
git log --oneline







Expected Output





1a5f3b2 (HEAD -> main) Add: filter_complete_cases to exclude missing grip strength values
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
Three commits, each documenting one analytical decision. A future collaborator can read this log and understand the analysis evolved step by step.



What you learned:

Each commit should represent one logical change with a meaningful message
git log --oneline gives a quick summary of history
Commit messages are your lab notebook — they document why decisions were made





Goal: Use git diff to compare different states of your repository.
Steps:

Modify analysis.R without staging:
echo "" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "# TODO: add regression model for primary outcome" >> scripts/analysis.R
See what changed (working directory vs last commit):
git diff







Expected Output





diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index a4f2c1d..b8e3f2a 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -4,3 +4,6 @@ normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100

 filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))
+
+# TODO: add regression model for primary outcome
The + lines are what you added. The context lines (no prefix) show where in the file the change occurred.




Stage the change and see how git diff output changes:
git add scripts/analysis.R
git diff          # no output — nothing left unstaged
git diff --staged # shows staged changes vs last commit







Expected Output of git diff --staged





diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index a4f2c1d..b8e3f2a 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -4,3 +4,6 @@ normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100

 filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))
+
+# TODO: add regression model for primary outcome
Same content — but now it shows staged vs committed, not working vs staged.




Compare the last two commits:
git diff HEAD~1 HEAD







Expected Output





diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index 9b2c3d1..a4f2c1d 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -2,3 +2,5 @@ library(dplyr)
 library(tidyr)

 normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
+
+filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))
HEAD~1 means “one commit before the current HEAD”. HEAD means the current commit.



What you learned:

git diff (no flags) shows unstaged changes
git diff --staged shows staged changes vs last commit
git diff HEAD~1 HEAD compares any two points in history







Common Mistakes




git diff shows nothing: Your changes are already staged. Use git diff --staged to see them.
git diff HEAD~1 HEAD shows nothing: You have only one commit — there is no HEAD~1 yet. Make at least 2 commits first.







Goal: Create a .gitignore that prevents data files from being tracked.
Steps:

Simulate creating a data file:
echo "participant_id,grip_strength,balance_score" > data/results.csv
echo "001,34.2,78.9" >> data/results.csv
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  ...

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        data/results.csv
        scripts/analysis.R
The CSV file appears as untracked. We do not want Git to track it.




Create a .gitignore:
nano .gitignore
Type the following, then press Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X:
data/
*.csv
*.RData
.env
Check status again:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  ...
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        .gitignore
data/results.csv has disappeared from the output — Git is now ignoring it. The .gitignore file itself is untracked and should be committed.




Commit the .gitignore:
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Add: .gitignore to exclude data files and credentials"
git log --oneline







Expected Output





b3a2c1d (HEAD -> main) Add: .gitignore to exclude data files and credentials
1a5f3b2 Add: filter_complete_cases to exclude missing grip strength values
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr



What you learned:

.gitignore prevents files matching the patterns from being tracked
data/ (with trailing slash) ignores the entire directory and its contents
The .gitignore file itself should be committed so collaborators get the same exclusions

text:

You can now:

title: Exercises: Module 04 — Branching
text:
Complete Module 04 and Exercises 03.



Goal: Create a feature branch, make a commit on it, switch back to main, and visualise the diverging history.
Steps:

Confirm you are on main with a clean state:
cd ~/rehab-research
git switch main
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean




Create and switch to a new feature branch:
git switch -c feature/add-grip-strength-measure







Expected Output





Switched to a new branch 'feature/add-grip-strength-measure'




Verify the new branch file in .git:
ls .git/refs/heads/feature/
cat .git/refs/heads/feature/add-grip-strength-measure
cat .git/refs/heads/main







Expected Output





add-grip-strength-measure
1a5f3b2c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a
1a5f3b2c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a
Both files contain the same hash — the branch just created points to the same commit as main. They will diverge as you make commits.




Add a function to analysis.R:
echo "" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "measure_grip_strength <- function(hand, trials) mean(trials[hand])" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: measure_grip_strength function for dominant/non-dominant hand"
Switch back to main and confirm the function is NOT there:
git switch main
cat scripts/analysis.R







Expected Output





The file should NOT contain measure_grip_strength. That function only exists on the feature branch. The working directory reflects whichever branch you are on.




Visualise the history:
git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate







Expected Output





* 3c4d5e6 (feature/add-grip-strength-measure) Add: measure_grip_strength function
* 1a5f3b2 (HEAD -> main, origin/main) Fix: correct normalization formula
* 9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
* 3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
The feature branch is one commit ahead of main. HEAD is on main.



What you learned:

git switch -c creates and switches in one step
A branch is a file in .git/refs/heads/ containing a commit hash

Goal: Create a second branch and inspect the branch files directly.
Steps:

Create a second branch (stay on main):
git switch -c fix/normalize-balance-scores
List all branch files:
ls .git/refs/heads/
ls .git/refs/heads/feature/







Expected Output





feature/  fix/  main

add-grip-strength-measure
Three branch directories/files now exist.




Read each branch file:
cat .git/refs/heads/main
cat .git/refs/heads/fix/normalize-balance-scores







Expected Output





Both files contain the same commit hash — fix/normalize-balance-scores was created from main and has not diverged yet.




Read HEAD to confirm your current branch:
cat .git/HEAD







Expected Output





ref: refs/heads/fix/normalize-balance-scores




Switch back to main:
git switch main
git branch -v







Expected Output





  feature/add-grip-strength-measure 3c4d5e6 Add: measure_grip_strength function
  fix/normalize-balance-scores       1a5f3b2 Fix: correct normalization formula
* main                               1a5f3b2 Fix: correct normalization formula
The * shows your current branch. The feature branch is one commit ahead.

title: Exercises: Module 06 — Rebase
text:
Complete Module 06 and Exercises 05. You should be on main in ~/rehab-research/ with a clean working tree.



Goal: Practice using interactive rebase to clean up WIP commits into research-quality commit messages before sharing.
Setup: Create a branch with exploratory commits that simulate real research iteration.

Create a new branch for exploratory analysis:
cd ~/rehab-research
git switch -c feature/exploratory-analysis
Create 6 small WIP commits:
echo "# explore grip" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "WIP"

echo "# try sd" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "still working"

echo "# maybe z-score" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "trying this"

echo "# reference Smith 2024" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "maybe"

echo "# confirmed formula" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "getting closer"

echo "explore_grip <- function(x) scale(x)" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "done"
Verify the messy history:
git log --oneline







Expected Output





f1e2d3c done
e2d3c4b getting closer
d3c4b5a maybe
c4b5a6d trying this
b5a6d7e still working
a6d7e8f WIP
1a5f3b2 (main) Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
...
Six WIP commits sit on top of the last main commit.




Start interactive rebase to squash the 6 commits into 2:
git rebase -i HEAD~6
Nano opens with the todo list. Change it to:
pick a6d7e8f WIP
squash b5a6d7e still working
squash c4b5a6d trying this
squash d3c4b5a maybe
pick e2d3c4b getting closer
squash f1e2d3c done
Save: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X
Git opens an editor for the first combined commit message. Replace all existing lines with:
Explore: investigate z-score normalization approaches for grip strength
Save and exit.
Git opens a second editor for the second combined commit message. Replace with:
Add: explore_grip function using scale() for z-score normalization
Save and exit.
Verify the clean history:
git log --oneline







Expected Output





9a8b7c6 Add: explore_grip function using scale() for z-score normalization
4d5e6f7 Explore: investigate z-score normalization approaches for grip strength
1a5f3b2 (main) Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
...
Six commits became two — the history now reads like a lab notebook, not a draft of notes.









Common Mistakes




Accidentally dropping a commit: If you delete a line from the rebase todo list, that commit’s changes are dropped permanently. Use drop explicitly when you intend to remove changes.
Exiting the editor without saving: On nano, Ctrl+C cancels without saving. The rebase aborts and asks you to resolve. Run git rebase --abort to return to the pre-rebase state.
Confusing squash and fixup: squash keeps both messages (and opens an editor to combine them). fixup keeps only the first message silently. Use fixup when the later commits are trivial fixes with no useful message.







Goal: Fix commit messages that do not follow the research lab format, using reword in interactive rebase.
Steps:

Check your current history on feature/exploratory-analysis:
git log --oneline -5
Start an interactive rebase to reword the top 2 commits:
git rebase -i HEAD~2
Change both pick to reword:
reword 9a8b7c6 Add: explore_grip function using scale() for z-score normalization
reword 4d5e6f7 Explore: investigate z-score normalization approaches for grip strength
Save and exit.
Git opens an editor for the first commit. Improve the message to include context:
Add: explore_grip() function using scale() — matches Smith et al. (2024) protocol
Save and exit.
Git opens an editor for the second commit. Improve it:
Explore: compare min-max, z-score, and scale() normalization for grip strength index
Save and exit.
Verify the improved messages:
git log --oneline -3







Expected Output





b3c4d5e Add: explore_grip() function using scale() — matches Smith et al. (2024) protocol
7e8f9a0 Explore: compare min-max, z-score, and scale() normalization for grip strength index
1a5f3b2 (main) Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
Both messages now follow the Type: description — context format with research references.



What you learned:

git rebase -i HEAD~N opens an interactive editor for the top N commits
squash folds a commit into the previous one (combines messages)
fixup folds a commit silently (discards its message)
reword lets you edit a message without touching the content
Interactive rebase rewrites hashes — only use it on branches you have not shared

title: Exercises: Module 08 — Data Management
text:
Complete Module 08 and Exercises 02. You should have a ~/rehab-research/ repository with a clean working tree.






Data Safety Checkpoint



Before proceeding: run git status and confirm you have no uncommitted data files. These exercises create files that simulate participant data — they will be cleaned up by .gitignore configurations you write.





Goal: Create realistic research data files and configure .gitignore to exclude them.
Steps:

Navigate to your repository and create simulated data files:
cd ~/rehab-research
mkdir -p data
echo "id,grip_kg,balance_score" > data/results.csv
echo "1001,28.4,72.3" >> data/results.csv
echo "SECRET_KEY=abc123" > .env
echo ".RData" > .RData
Check the current status — all three files appear as untracked:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        .RData
        .env
        data/

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
All three are visible — Git does not know to exclude them yet.




Create .gitignore with research-appropriate patterns:
nano .gitignore
Add the following lines:
data/
.env
*.RData
Save: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X
Check status again:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        .gitignore

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
data/, .env, and .RData no longer appear. Only .gitignore itself is untracked.




Commit the .gitignore:
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Data: add .gitignore to protect participant data and credentials"







Common Mistakes




Forgetting the trailing / on directory names: data (no slash) matches a file named data OR a directory. data/ explicitly targets the directory. Both work, but data/ is clearer.
Path separator on Windows: .gitignore uses forward slashes (data/) even on Windows with Git Bash. Backslashes (data\) do not work in .gitignore.
Quoting the pattern: echo "data/" >> .gitignore is correct. Do not add quotes inside the file itself — "data/" (with quotes) would match a directory literally named "data/" (including quote characters).







Goal: Simulate the common mistake of committing data before .gitignore is configured, then correctly remove it from version control while keeping it on disk.
Setup: First, create a situation where data was committed accidentally.

Temporarily remove .gitignore for this simulation:
git rm .gitignore
git commit -m "Temp: remove gitignore (simulation only)"
Now commit a data file (simulating the mistake):
echo "id,name,dob" > data/participants.csv
echo "1001,Smith,1985-03-14" >> data/participants.csv
git add data/participants.csv
git commit -m "Add: participant data file"
Verify the file is tracked:
git log --oneline -- data/participants.csv







Expected Output





a1b2c3d Add: participant data file
The file appears in history — this is the problem we are solving.




Remove from version control (but keep on disk):
git rm --cached data/participants.csv







Expected Output





rm 'data/participants.csv'




Verify the file still exists on disk:
ls data/







Expected Output





participants.csv
The file is still there — --cached only removed it from Git’s index.




Re-add .gitignore with data/ pattern:
nano .gitignore
Add:
data/
.env
*.RData
Save and exit.
Check status and commit:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        deleted:    data/participants.csv

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        .gitignore
data/participants.csv is staged for deletion from version control. .gitignore is new and untracked.



git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Data: remove participant data from version control tracking"

Confirm the file is gone from current tracking but still on disk:
git ls-files data/
ls data/







Expected Output





(empty — git ls-files shows nothing in data/)

participants.csv
git ls-files lists only files Git is tracking — data/participants.csv is no longer tracked. The ls data/ confirms it is still physically present on disk.









The Commit Is Still in History



Even after this exercise, data/participants.csv exists in the commit "Add: participant data file". Anyone with access to this repository can run git show on that hash and read the file content.
In a real scenario with actual PHI: immediately contact your institution’s data security office. Removing from history requires git filter-repo and force-pushing — actions that affect every collaborator and require coordination.






Goal: Use Git inspection commands to understand what is stored in your repository and identify potential problems before publication.
Steps:

Check the repository size:
cd ~/rehab-research
git count-objects -vH







Expected Output





count: 28
size: 89.50 KiB
in-pack: 0
packs: 0
size-pack: 0 bytes
prune-packable: 0
garbage: 0
size-garbage: 0 bytes
count is the number of loose objects. size is their total disk usage. Your numbers will differ depending on how many commits you have made. For a code-only research repository, this should stay under a few hundred KiB.




Search history for any CSV files that were ever committed:
git log --all --full-history -- "*.csv"







Expected Output (if you followed Exercise 8.2)





a1b2c3d Add: participant data file
This shows the commit where data/participants.csv was accidentally committed. Even though it is no longer tracked, it remains in history.
If this output is empty, no CSV was ever committed — your repository is clean.




List all currently tracked files:
git ls-files







Expected Output





.gitignore
scripts/analysis.R
Only files that Git is actively tracking appear. If you see anything in data/, that file is still tracked and needs git rm --cached treatment.




Check for common PHI-related filename patterns in tracked files:
git ls-files | grep -iE "participant|subject|patient|dob|phi" && echo "FOUND potential PHI filenames" || echo "No PHI-related filenames found"







Expected Output (after Exercise 8.2)





No PHI-related filenames found
The grep returns nothing because participants.csv is no longer tracked. The || (OR operator) runs the echo when grep finds no matches.




Verify .gitignore is correctly excluding the data directory:
git check-ignore -v data/participants.csv







Expected Output





.gitignore:1:data/  data/participants.csv
This shows: file .gitignore, line 1, pattern data/ is ignoring data/participants.csv. The git check-ignore -v command explains exactly which .gitignore rule is responsible for ignoring a file — useful for debugging unexpected ignore behavior.



What you learned:

.gitignore prevents files from being staged — but only if they are not already tracked
git rm --cached removes a file from tracking while keeping it on disk
Past commits retain file content even after .gitignore is configured
git count-objects -vH, git ls-files, and git log --all -- pattern give you a complete picture of what is stored in your repository







Common Mistakes




Using git rm without --cached: This deletes the file from disk AND stops tracking it. Always use --cached when you want to keep the file.
Running git add . after setting up .gitignore: Generally safe once .gitignore is in place, but double-check git status output before committing to confirm no sensitive files slipped through.
Assuming .gitignore fixes past commits: It does not. History is permanent until you take explicit destructive action with git filter-repo.

The file is still there — --cached only removed it from Git’s index.




Re-add .gitignore with data/ pattern:
nano .gitignore
Add:
data/
.env
*.RData
Save and exit.
Check status and commit:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        deleted:    data/participants.csv

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        .gitignore
data/participants.csv is staged for deletion from version control. .gitignore is new and untracked.



git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Data: remove participant data from version control tracking"

Confirm the file is gone from current tracking but still on disk:
git ls-files data/
ls data/







Expected Output





(empty — git ls-files shows nothing in data/)

participants.csv
git ls-files lists only files Git is tracking — data/participants.csv is no longer tracked. The ls data/ confirms it is still physically present on disk.









The Commit Is Still in History



Even after this exercise, data/participants.csv exists in the commit "Add: participant data file". Anyone with access to this repository can run git show on that hash and read the file content.
In a real scenario with actual PHI: immediately contact your institution’s data security office. Removing from history requires git filter-repo and force-pushing — actions that affect every collaborator and require coordination.






Goal: Use Git inspection commands to understand what is stored in your repository and identify potential problems before publication.
Steps:

Check the repository size:
cd ~/rehab-research
git count-objects -vH







Expected Output





count: 28
size: 89.50 KiB
in-pack: 0
packs: 0
size-pack: 0 bytes
prune-packable: 0
garbage: 0
size-garbage: 0 bytes
count is the number of loose objects. size is their total disk usage. Your numbers will differ depending on how many commits you have made. For a code-only research repository, this should stay under a few hundred KiB.




Search history for any CSV files that were ever committed:
git log --all --full-history -- "*.csv"







Expected Output (if you followed Exercise 8.2)





a1b2c3d Add: participant data file
This shows the commit where data/participants.csv was accidentally committed. Even though it is no longer tracked, it remains in history.
If this output is empty, no CSV was ever committed — your repository is clean.




List all currently tracked files:
git ls-files







Expected Output





.gitignore
scripts/analysis.R
Only files that Git is actively tracking appear. If you see anything in data/, that file is still tracked and needs git rm --cached treatment.




Check for common PHI-related filename patterns in tracked files:
git ls-files | grep -iE "participant|subject|patient|dob|phi" && echo "FOUND potential PHI filenames" || echo "No PHI-related filenames found"







Expected Output (after Exercise 8.2)





No PHI-related filenames found
The grep returns nothing because participants.csv is no longer tracked. The || (OR operator) runs the echo when grep finds no matches.




Verify .gitignore is correctly excluding the data directory:
git check-ignore -v data/participants.csv







Expected Output





.gitignore:1:data/  data/participants.csv
This shows: file .gitignore, line 1, pattern data/ is ignoring data/participants.csv. The git check-ignore -v command explains exactly which .gitignore rule is responsible for ignoring a file — useful for debugging unexpected ignore behavior.



What you learned:

.gitignore prevents files from being staged — but only if they are not already tracked
git rm --cached removes a file from tracking while keeping it on disk
Past commits retain file content even after .gitignore is configured
git count-objects -vH, git ls-files, and git log --all -- pattern give you a complete picture of what is stored in your repository







Common Mistakes




Using git rm without --cached: This deletes the file from disk AND stops tracking it. Always use --cached when you want to keep the file.
Running git add . after setting up .gitignore: Generally safe once .gitignore is in place, but double-check git status output before committing to confirm no sensitive files slipped through.
Assuming .gitignore fixes past commits: It does not. History is permanent until you take explicit destructive action with git filter-repo.

title: Exercises: Module 10 — Troubleshooting and Recovery
text:
Complete Module 10 and Exercises 02. You should have ~/rehab-research/ with several commits in history.






These Exercises Involve Destructive Commands



Exercise 10.1 enters detached HEAD. Exercise 10.2 uses git reset. Exercise 10.3 uses git restore. All of these are recoverable within the exercise — but read each step carefully and do not skip ahead.





Goal: Deliberately enter detached HEAD state, make a commit, then rescue that commit onto a branch before switching away.
Steps:

Confirm you are on main with a clean state:
cd ~/rehab-research
git switch main
git status
Find a commit hash to check out (two commits back):
git log --oneline -5







Expected Output





3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script
...
Note the hash of the second commit (e.g., 9d4c7e1).




Check out that commit by hash (this detaches HEAD):
git checkout 9d4c7e1







Expected Output





Note: switching to '9d4c7e1'.

You are in 'detached HEAD' state. You can look around, make experimental
changes and commit them, but if you switch to another branch, those commits
may be lost and Git will tell you about it with 'git switch -c <new-branch-name>'.

HEAD is now at 9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function




Confirm detached HEAD with git status:
git status







Expected Output





HEAD detached at 9d4c7e1
nothing to commit, working tree clean
No branch name — just “HEAD detached at HASH”.




Make a commit while in detached HEAD:
echo "# Experimental: test detached commit" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state"







Expected Output





[detached HEAD a1b2c3d] Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
The commit was created but it is on no branch.




Use git reflog to find the commit hash of what you just committed:
git reflog | head -5







Expected Output





a1b2c3d (HEAD) HEAD@{0}: commit: Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state
9d4c7e1 HEAD@{1}: checkout: moving from main to 9d4c7e1
3c4d5e6 (main) HEAD@{2}: commit: Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
The detached commit is at HEAD@{0} — hash a1b2c3d.




Rescue the commit onto a new branch before switching away:
git switch -c rescued-commit







Expected Output





Switched to a new branch 'rescued-commit'
HEAD is now attached to rescued-commit. The experimental commit is safe.




Verify:
git log --oneline
git status







Expected Output





a1b2c3d (HEAD -> rescued-commit) Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script

On branch rescued-commit
nothing to commit, working tree clean
The commit is on rescued-commit. Switch back to main to continue normal work:
git switch main









Common Mistakes




Switching to another branch before creating a rescue branch: If you run git switch main while in detached HEAD with commits, those commits become “dangling” — still in .git/objects/ but not reachable from any branch. You can still recover them via git reflog, but act quickly.







Goal: Make a commit with incorrect content, then use git reset --soft to undo the commit while keeping the changes staged for recommitting correctly.
Steps:

Accidentally commit with the wrong message and mixed content:
cd ~/rehab-research
git switch main
echo "grip_score <- 1 + 1  # wrong formula" >> scripts/analysis.R
echo "# TODO: remove this" >> scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "asdfasdf fix stuff"
Check the bad commit:
git log --oneline -3







Expected Output





b3c4d5e asdfasdf fix stuff
3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
The top commit has a poor message and mixed content.




Undo the commit but keep the changes staged:
git reset HEAD~1 --soft
Verify the commit is gone but changes are staged:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
The commit is undone. The changes are staged, ready to be split and recommitted properly.




Unstage and inspect what was added:
git restore --staged scripts/analysis.R
git diff scripts/analysis.R







Expected Output





diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index a1b2c3d..4e5f6a7 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -N,N +N,N @@
+grip_score <- 1 + 1  # wrong formula
+# TODO: remove this
The two lines are visible as unstaged changes. You can now decide what to keep and what to discard.




Discard the bad lines and make a clean commit:
git restore scripts/analysis.R
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
The bad changes are gone. The repository is back to a clean state before the mistake.



What you learned:

git reset HEAD~1 --soft undoes the last commit and leaves changes staged
git restore --staged unstages without discarding working directory changes
git restore FILE discards working directory changes (permanent — use carefully)





Goal: Accidentally destructively edit a file without staging, then use git restore to recover the last committed version.
Steps:

Make a destructive change to analysis.R — overwrite it entirely:
cd ~/rehab-research
echo "rm -rf everything" > scripts/analysis.R
cat scripts/analysis.R







Expected Output





rm -rf everything
The file now contains only this line — all previous content is gone from the working directory.




Check git status:
git status







Expected Output





On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
The file is modified but not staged. Git’s hint already tells you the recovery command.




Restore the last committed version:
git restore scripts/analysis.R
Verify recovery:
cat scripts/analysis.R
git status







Expected Output





library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))

...

On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
The original content is fully restored from the last commit. The destructive change is gone.









git restore Is Permanent for Unstaged Changes



The destructive change in this exercise (echo "rm -rf everything" > analysis.R) was only in your working directory — never staged or committed. Once you ran git restore, it was permanently discarded with no way to recover it.
This is the intended behavior — but it means you should always check git diff before running git restore, to confirm you are not discarding work you need.


What you learned:

git restore FILE restores a file to its last committed state
This is permanent for unstaged changes — there is no undo
Always check git diff before restoring to confirm you want to discard the changes

title: Exercises: Module 12 — Professional Research Repositories
text:
Complete Module 12 and Exercises 08. This is the capstone exercise — you will build a complete, publication-ready repository from scratch.



Goal: Create a complete research repository for a grip strength study, applying everything learned across all 13 modules.
Steps:

Create a fresh repository:
mkdir ~/grip-strength-study
cd ~/grip-strength-study
git init
git config user.name "Your Name"
git config user.email "your@institution.edu"







Expected Output





Initialized empty Git repository in /Users/jbayer/grip-strength-study/.git/




Create the full directory structure:
mkdir -p scripts data figures docs
touch data/.gitkeep figures/.gitkeep
Write README.md with all required sections:
nano README.md
Write:
# Grip Strength Recovery in Stroke Rehabilitation

Analysis code for the study examining bilateral grip strength asymmetry
as a predictor of functional recovery in first-time stroke survivors.

## Installation

```bash
git clone git@github.com:yourusername/grip-strength-study.git
cd grip-strength-study
Rscript -e "renv::restore()"

Data files are not included (participant privacy). Place your processed data in data/ following docs/codebook.md.
Rscript scripts/analysis.R
Rscript scripts/figures.R

Raw data: restricted access via institutional archive. Contact: your@institution.edu

Bayer, J. et al. (2026). Grip strength asymmetry as predictor of recovery in stroke rehabilitation. Neurorehabilitation, XX, XXX-XXX.

Code: MIT License (see LICENSE). ```
Save: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X
Add the MIT License:
cat > LICENSE << 'EOF'
MIT License

Copyright (c) 2026 Your Name

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
copies or substantial portions of the Software.

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.
EOF
Create .gitignore with research patterns:
cat > .gitignore << 'EOF'
data/
figures/
*.RData
*.csv
.env
.Rhistory
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
renv/library/
EOF
Create the main analysis script:
cat > scripts/analysis.R << 'EOF'
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# Grip strength asymmetry index
# |dominant - non-dominant| / mean(dominant, non-dominant)
grip_asymmetry <- function(dominant, non_dominant) {
  abs(dominant - non_dominant) / ((dominant + non_dominant) / 2)
}

# Z-score normalization
normalize_grip <- function(x) (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)

# Filter complete grip strength cases
filter_complete_grip <- function(df) {
  dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_dominant), !is.na(grip_non_dominant))
}
EOF
Create a codebook:
cat > docs/codebook.md << 'EOF'
# Data Codebook — Grip Strength Study

## Variables

| Variable | Type | Units | Description |
|----------|------|-------|-------------|
| id | integer | — | Participant ID (anonymized) |
| age | integer | years | Age at enrollment |
| grip_dominant | numeric | kg | Dominant hand grip strength (mean of 3 trials) |
| grip_non_dominant | numeric | kg | Non-dominant hand grip strength (mean of 3 trials) |
| balance_score | numeric | 0–100 | Berg Balance Scale score |
| days_post_stroke | integer | days | Days between stroke onset and enrollment |

## Missing Values

Missing values are coded as NA. Use `filter_complete_grip()` before analysis.
EOF
Make three structured commits:
# Commit 1: project structure
git add README.md LICENSE .gitignore data/.gitkeep figures/.gitkeep
git commit -m "Structure: initialize grip strength study repository"

# Commit 2: analysis code
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: core analysis functions (grip_asymmetry, normalize_grip, filter_complete_grip)"

# Commit 3: documentation
git add docs/codebook.md
git commit -m "Docs: add variable codebook for data contributors"
Verify the clean history:
git log --oneline







Expected Output





c4d5e6f Docs: add variable codebook for data contributors
b3c4d5e Add: core analysis functions (grip_asymmetry, normalize_grip, filter_complete_grip)
a2b3c4d Structure: initialize grip strength study repository




Tag the first release:
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Wave 1 analysis — manuscript submission ready"
Verify the tag:
git show v1.0.0







Expected Output





tag v1.0.0
Tagger: Your Name <your@institution.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 17:00:00 2026 +0100

Wave 1 analysis — manuscript submission ready

commit c4d5e6f...
Author: Your Name <your@institution.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 16:55:00 2026 +0100

    Docs: add variable codebook for data contributors
...




Create the release archive:
git archive --format=zip v1.0.0 > grip-strength-v1.0.0.zip
ls -lh grip-strength-v1.0.0.zip







Expected Output





-rw-r--r--  1 jbayer  staff   3.2K Feb 28 17:00 grip-strength-v1.0.0.zip
A small archive — only tracked source files, no data, no .git/ history.









Common Mistakes




Tagging the wrong commit: Always confirm git log --oneline shows exactly the commits you want before tagging. Once pushed, a tag pointing to the wrong commit is awkward to fix (requires deleting and re-creating the tag on the remote).
Forgetting to push the tag: git push origin main does NOT push tags. Use git push origin v1.0.0 or git push origin --tags.
Including data in the archive: git archive includes everything that is tracked. If you accidentally tracked a data file, it will appear in the archive. Always run git ls-files before archiving to confirm no data files are tracked.







Goal: Verify your new repository passes all eight pre-publication checks.
Steps:
Run each check and record the result:

Repository size:
git count-objects -vH







Expected Output





count: 12
size: 12.50 KiB
in-pack: 0
packs: 0
size-pack: 0 bytes
Under 1 MB total — healthy for a code-only repository.




Data directory excluded:
grep "^data" .gitignore && echo "PASS: data/ pattern found" || echo "FAIL: no data/ pattern"







Expected Output





data/
PASS: data/ pattern found




All tracked files (verify no data files):
git ls-files







Expected Output





.gitignore
LICENSE
README.md
data/.gitkeep
docs/codebook.md
figures/.gitkeep
scripts/analysis.R
data/.gitkeep is a zero-byte file — acceptable. No .csv, no participant data.




CSV history (check if any CSV was ever committed):
git log --all --full-history -- "*.csv" | head -5
echo "(empty = PASS: no CSV ever committed)"







Expected Output





(empty = PASS: no CSV ever committed)




.env history (check if any .env was ever committed):
git log --all --full-history -- ".env" | head -5
echo "(empty = PASS: no .env ever committed)"







Expected Output





(empty = PASS: no .env ever committed)




README present:
git ls-files README.md && echo "PASS: README.md committed" || echo "FAIL: no README.md"







Expected Output





README.md
PASS: README.md committed




LICENSE present:
git ls-files LICENSE && echo "PASS: LICENSE committed" || echo "FAIL: no LICENSE"







Expected Output





LICENSE
PASS: LICENSE committed




Version tag exists:
git tag && echo "PASS: tag(s) exist" || echo "FAIL: no tags found"







Expected Output





v1.0.0
PASS: tag(s) exist



All 8 checks pass — your repository is publication-ready.
What you learned:

A publication-ready repository requires: README, LICENSE, .gitignore, structured commits, and a version tag
The pre-publication checklist catches common mistakes before they become public
git archive creates a clean snapshot without .git/ history for submission
