## Installation

```bash
git clone git@github.com:yourusername/grip-strength-study.git
cd grip-strength-study
Rscript -e "renv::restore()"

Data files are not included (participant privacy). Place your processed data in data/ following docs/codebook.md.
Rscript scripts/analysis.R
Rscript scripts/figures.R

Raw data: restricted access via institutional archive. Contact: your@institution.edu

Bayer, J. et al. (2026). Grip strength asymmetry as predictor of recovery in stroke rehabilitation. Neurorehabilitation, XX, XXX-XXX.

Code: MIT License (see LICENSE). ```
Save: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X
Add the MIT License:
cat > LICENSE << 'EOF'
MIT License

Copyright (c) 2026 Your Name

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
copies or substantial portions of the Software.

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.
EOF
Create .gitignore with research patterns:
cat > .gitignore << 'EOF'
data/
figures/
*.RData
*.csv
.env
.Rhistory
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
renv/library/
EOF
Create the main analysis script:
cat > scripts/analysis.R << 'EOF'
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# Grip strength asymmetry index
# |dominant - non-dominant| / mean(dominant, non-dominant)
grip_asymmetry <- function(dominant, non_dominant) {
  abs(dominant - non_dominant) / ((dominant + non_dominant) / 2)
}

# Z-score normalization
normalize_grip <- function(x) (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)

# Filter complete grip strength cases
filter_complete_grip <- function(df) {
  dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_dominant), !is.na(grip_non_dominant))
}
EOF
Create a codebook:
cat > docs/codebook.md << 'EOF'
# Data Codebook — Grip Strength Study

## Variables

| Variable | Type | Units | Description |
|----------|------|-------|-------------|
| id | integer | — | Participant ID (anonymized) |
| age | integer | years | Age at enrollment |
| grip_dominant | numeric | kg | Dominant hand grip strength (mean of 3 trials) |
| grip_non_dominant | numeric | kg | Non-dominant hand grip strength (mean of 3 trials) |
| balance_score | numeric | 0–100 | Berg Balance Scale score |
| days_post_stroke | integer | days | Days between stroke onset and enrollment |

## Missing Values

Missing values are coded as NA. Use `filter_complete_grip()` before analysis.
EOF
Make three structured commits:
# Commit 1: project structure
git add README.md LICENSE .gitignore data/.gitkeep figures/.gitkeep
git commit -m "Structure: initialize grip strength study repository"

# Commit 2: analysis code
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add: core analysis functions (grip_asymmetry, normalize_grip, filter_complete_grip)"

# Commit 3: documentation
git add docs/codebook.md
git commit -m "Docs: add variable codebook for data contributors"
Verify the clean history:
git log --oneline







Expected Output





c4d5e6f Docs: add variable codebook for data contributors
b3c4d5e Add: core analysis functions (grip_asymmetry, normalize_grip, filter_complete_grip)
a2b3c4d Structure: initialize grip strength study repository




Tag the first release:
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Wave 1 analysis — manuscript submission ready"
Verify the tag:
git show v1.0.0







Expected Output





tag v1.0.0
Tagger: Your Name <your@institution.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 17:00:00 2026 +0100

Wave 1 analysis — manuscript submission ready

commit c4d5e6f...
Author: Your Name <your@institution.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 16:55:00 2026 +0100

    Docs: add variable codebook for data contributors
...




Create the release archive:
git archive --format=zip v1.0.0 > grip-strength-v1.0.0.zip
ls -lh grip-strength-v1.0.0.zip







Expected Output





-rw-r--r--  1 jbayer  staff   3.2K Feb 28 17:00 grip-strength-v1.0.0.zip
A small archive — only tracked source files, no data, no .git/ history.









Common Mistakes




Tagging the wrong commit: Always confirm git log --oneline shows exactly the commits you want before tagging. Once pushed, a tag pointing to the wrong commit is awkward to fix (requires deleting and re-creating the tag on the remote).
Forgetting to push the tag: git push origin main does NOT push tags. Use git push origin v1.0.0 or git push origin --tags.
Including data in the archive: git archive includes everything that is tracked. If you accidentally tracked a data file, it will appear in the archive. Always run git ls-files before archiving to confirm no data files are tracked.







Goal: Verify your new repository passes all eight pre-publication checks.
Steps:
Run each check and record the result:

Repository size:
git count-objects -vH







Expected Output





count: 12
size: 12.50 KiB
in-pack: 0
packs: 0
size-pack: 0 bytes
Under 1 MB total — healthy for a code-only repository.




Data directory excluded:
grep "^data" .gitignore && echo "PASS: data/ pattern found" || echo "FAIL: no data/ pattern"







Expected Output





data/
PASS: data/ pattern found




All tracked files (verify no data files):
git ls-files







Expected Output





.gitignore
LICENSE
README.md
data/.gitkeep
docs/codebook.md
figures/.gitkeep
scripts/analysis.R
data/.gitkeep is a zero-byte file — acceptable. No .csv, no participant data.




CSV history (check if any CSV was ever committed):
git log --all --full-history -- "*.csv" | head -5
echo "(empty = PASS: no CSV ever committed)"







Expected Output





(empty = PASS: no CSV ever committed)




.env history (check if any .env was ever committed):
git log --all --full-history -- ".env" | head -5
echo "(empty = PASS: no .env ever committed)"







Expected Output





(empty = PASS: no .env ever committed)




README present:
git ls-files README.md && echo "PASS: README.md committed" || echo "FAIL: no README.md"







Expected Output





README.md
PASS: README.md committed




LICENSE present:
git ls-files LICENSE && echo "PASS: LICENSE committed" || echo "FAIL: no LICENSE"







Expected Output





LICENSE
PASS: LICENSE committed




Version tag exists:
git tag && echo "PASS: tag(s) exist" || echo "FAIL: no tags found"







Expected Output





v1.0.0
PASS: tag(s) exist



All 8 checks pass — your repository is publication-ready.
What you learned:

A publication-ready repository requires: README, LICENSE, .gitignore, structured commits, and a version tag
The pre-publication checklist catches common mistakes before they become public
git archive creates a clean snapshot without .git/ history for submission

title: Exercises: Module 12 — Professional Research Repositories
text:
Summary
You have completed Module 12 and all 13 modules of the Advanced_Git course.
You can now:

Build a research repository with professional structure from scratch
Write README and LICENSE files from the terminal
Apply semantic versioning with annotated tags
Create release archives with git archive
Complete a pre-publication safety checklist







Congratulations



You have the skills to manage a research project from the first git init through manuscript submission — entirely from the terminal.
Reference materials:

Command Index — all commands in one place
Error Messages — quick troubleshooting lookup
Cheat Sheet — printable two-column reference

title: Module 00: Terminal Fundamentals
text:
The terminal is a text interface for giving your computer instructions. Instead of clicking icons, you type commands. This module teaches you everything you need to use the terminal confidently — before we touch Git at all.
By the end of this module, you will be able to navigate your computer’s file system, create the project folder for this course, and understand what every part of a terminal command means.

title: Module 00: Terminal Fundamentals
text:
The terminal is a text interface for giving your computer instructions. Instead of clicking icons, you type commands. This module teaches you everything you need to use the terminal confidently — before we touch Git at all.
By the end of this module, you will be able to navigate your computer’s file system, create the project folder for this course, and understand what every part of a terminal command means.

title: Module 00: Terminal Fundamentals
text:
Prerequisites
None. This is the starting point.
Before beginning, make sure Git and a terminal are installed on your machine. See the setup guide for your operating system:

macOS Setup Guide
Linux Setup Guide
Windows Setup Guide



What Is the Terminal?
The terminal (also called the command line, shell, or console) is a text-based interface for communicating with your operating system. Instead of pointing and clicking, you type instructions called commands.
When you open the terminal, you see something like this:
yourname@hostname ~ $
Each part has a meaning:
yourname   @   hostname   ~   $
   │            │         │   │
   │            │         │   └─ prompt: the terminal is ready for your input
   │            │         └───── current directory (~ means your home directory)
   │            └─────────────── your computer's name
   └──────────────────────────── your username
The $ symbol at the end is called the prompt. It means “I’m ready — type a command.” In this course, we always use $ as the generic prompt symbol regardless of your shell.






Tip



Windows note (Git Bash): Your prompt will look like yourname@HOSTNAME MINGW64 ~\n$. The MINGW64 part just means you are using Git Bash — ignore it.





Opening a Terminal

macOSLinuxWindows (Git Bash)Windows (WSL2)


Press Command + Space, type Terminal, and press Return.
Alternatively: Applications → Utilities → Terminal.


Press Ctrl + Alt + T (most distributions), or search “Terminal” in your application launcher.


Search Git Bash in the Start menu and open it.


Search Ubuntu (or your chosen distro) in the Start menu and open it.






Basic Navigation
These three commands let you move around the file system. You will use them constantly — every Git session starts with navigation.

pwd — Print Working Directory
pwd: displays your current location in the file system.
pwd
/Users/yourname
This is your home directory — the starting point for your user account. The path /Users/yourname means: start at the root /, go into Users/, then yourname/.


ls — List Directory Contents
ls: lists the files and folders in the current directory.
ls
Desktop    Documents    Downloads    Movies    Music    Pictures
Add -la to see hidden files and detailed information:
ls -la
total 0
drwxr-xr-x   8 yourname  staff   256 Feb 28 09:00 .
drwxr-xr-x   6 root      wheel   192 Feb 28 09:00 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   220 Feb 28 09:00 .bash_profile
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff    64 Feb 28 09:00 Desktop
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff    64 Feb 28 09:00 Documents
The -l flag shows permissions, owner, size, and modification date. The -a flag shows hidden files (files starting with .).






Tip



Flags can be combined: -la is the same as -l -a.




cd — Change Directory
cd: moves you into a different directory.
cd Documents
(no output — success is silence)
pwd
/Users/yourname/Documents
To go back up one level:
cd ..
.. always means “the directory above the current one.”
To go directly to your home directory from anywhere:
cd ~
~ is a shortcut for your home directory (/Users/yourname on macOS, /home/yourname on Linux).




File Operations
These commands let you create, read, copy, move, and delete files — the building blocks of organising a research project.

mkdir — Make Directory
mkdir: creates a new folder.
mkdir rehab-research
ls
Desktop    Documents    Downloads    Movies    Music    Pictures    rehab-research
Use -p to create nested directories in one command:
mkdir -p rehab-research/scripts rehab-research/data rehab-research/docs


touch — Create an Empty File
touch: creates an empty file (or updates a file’s timestamp if it exists).
touch rehab-research/scripts/analysis.R


cat — Display File Contents
cat: prints the contents of a file to the terminal.
cat rehab-research/scripts/analysis.R
(empty — the file exists but has no content yet)


mv — Move or Rename
mv: moves a file to a different location, or renames it.
# Rename a file
mv analysis.R grip_strength_analysis.R

# Move a file into a directory
mv grip_strength_analysis.R rehab-research/scripts/


cp — Copy
cp: copies a file.
cp rehab-research/scripts/analysis.R rehab-research/scripts/analysis_backup.R


rm — Remove
rm: permanently deletes a file.
rm rehab-research/scripts/analysis_backup.R






rm is permanent



Unlike moving to the Trash, rm does not offer a recovery option. Double-check before running it. Add -i for confirmation prompts: rm -i filename






Paths: Absolute vs Relative
Every file on your computer has an absolute path — its complete address from the root of the file system.
# Absolute path (works from anywhere)
ls /Users/yourname/rehab-research/scripts

# Relative path (works from your home directory)
ls rehab-research/scripts
A relative path is relative to wherever you currently are. An absolute path starts with / (macOS/Linux) or C:\ (Windows) and works from anywhere.
Rule of thumb: Use relative paths when you are confident where you are. Use absolute paths when writing scripts or when in doubt.



Command Structure
Every terminal command follows the same pattern:
command   -flags   argument(s)
   │          │         │
   │          │         └── what the command acts on (a file, directory, etc.)
   │          └──────────── modify the command's behavior
   └─────────────────────── the action to perform
Example:
ls   -la   rehab-research/

ls — the command (list)
-la — flags: -l (long format), -a (show hidden files)
rehab-research/ — the argument (which directory to list)

Not all commands require flags or arguments. pwd works with none at all.



Getting Help
Two sources of documentation are always available in the terminal.

--help
Most commands accept a --help flag that prints a brief usage summary:
git --help
usage: git [-v | --version] [-h | --help] [-C <path>] ...

These are common Git commands used in various situations:

start a working area
   clone     Clone a repository into a new directory
   init      Create an empty Git repository or reinitialize...


man — Manual Pages
man opens the full documentation for a command:
man ls
Navigate with arrow keys. Press q to quit.






Tip



Windows (Git Bash): man may not be available. Use command --help instead, or search linux.die.net/man.






Common Error Messages
These five errors appear constantly. Learning to read them removes most of the anxiety of working in the terminal.

command not found
$ gti status
bash: gti: command not found
What it means: The program you typed does not exist (or is not on your PATH). Fix: Check spelling. The command is git, not gti.


No such file or directory
$ cd Documents/rehab-stuyd
cd: no such file or directory: Documents/rehab-stuyd
What it means: The path you typed does not exist. Fix: Run ls to check the actual name, then correct the typo.


Permission denied
$ rm /usr/local/bin/git
rm: /usr/local/bin/git: Permission denied
What it means: You do not have the rights to perform this action on that file. Fix: For system files, use sudo. For your own files, check ownership with ls -la.


Is a directory
$ cat rehab-research/
cat: rehab-research/: Is a directory
What it means: You used a file command on a folder. Fix: Use ls rehab-research/ to list the directory instead.


Not a directory
$ cd rehab-research/analysis.R
cd: not a directory: rehab-research/analysis.R
What it means: You tried to cd into a file, not a folder. Fix: Use cd rehab-research/ and then open the file with cat or nano.




Why Terminal for Research?
You may be wondering: my GUI works fine — why bother?
Reproducibility: Every action you take in a GUI is invisible to collaborators and to your future self. In the terminal, every command is text — it can be copied, saved to a script, shared in a methods section, and run again identically six months later.
Documentation: Your terminal history is a log of everything you did. history | grep git shows every Git command from your current session.
Automation: Once you know the commands, you can chain them into shell scripts that process entire datasets, push to servers, or generate figures — all from a single line.
Portability: Terminal commands work on macOS, Linux, and Windows (WSL/Git Bash) with minimal variation. A GUI installed on your laptop may not exist on your institution’s computing cluster or a collaborator’s machine. git always does.

What’s Next
In Module 01 — Git Concepts, you will learn what Git actually does, explore the .git directory from the terminal, and understand the mental model that makes everything else in this course click.

title: Module 02: Basic Git Terminal Workflow
text: Module 01 showed you what Git does internally. Now you will learn the five commands you will use every single day: git status, git add, git commit, git log, and git diff. By the end of this module, your research scripts will be in version control with a meaningful history.

title: Module 02: Basic Git Terminal Workflow
text: Module 01 showed you what Git does internally. Now you will learn the five commands you will use every single day: git status, git add, git commit, git log, and git diff. By the end of this module, your research scripts will be in version control with a meaningful history.

title: Module 02: Basic Git Terminal Workflow
text:
Prerequisites

Module 00 — Terminal Fundamentals
Module 01 — Git Concepts
~/rehab-research/ exists and has been initialized with git init



git init: Starting a Repository
If you have not already done so from Module 01 exercises:
cd ~/rehab-research
git init
Initialized empty Git repository in /Users/yourname/rehab-research/.git/
You only run git init once per project. After this, every git command you run inside rehab-research/ (and any subdirectory) will apply to this repository.



git status: Your Constant Companion
git status: shows the current state of your working directory and staging area.
Run it constantly — before and after every other Git command. It always tells you what to do next.
git status
Here are the five states you will encounter, with what each means:
Nothing to commit (clean):
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
Untracked files (Git sees them but is not tracking them):
On branch main
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        scripts/analysis.R
Changes not staged (tracked file has been modified):
On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
Changes staged for commit (ready to commit):
On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
Mixed state (some staged, some not):
On branch main
Changes to be committed:
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R

Changes not staged for commit:
        modified:   docs/protocol.md



git add: Staging Changes
git add: moves a file from the working directory into the staging area.
git add scripts/analysis.R   # stage one specific file
git add scripts/             # stage all files in a directory
git add .                    # stage all changes in the current directory






Tip



Prefer git add scripts/analysis.R (specific file) over git add . (everything). Staging specifically forces you to think about what is going into each commit — a good habit that prevents accidentally committing data files or credentials.


What staging really does: When you run git add, Git computes the SHA-1 hash of the file’s contents and stores a blob object in .git/objects/. The staging area (index file) records this hash alongside the file’s path and metadata.
ls .git/objects/   # before git add
info  pack
git add scripts/analysis.R
ls .git/objects/   # after git add
1a  info  pack
A new subdirectory appeared — that is your file’s blob object.



git commit: Creating Snapshots
git commit: takes everything in the staging area and creates a permanent snapshot.
git commit -m "Add initial dplyr data cleaning script"
[main (root-commit) 3f2a1b4] Add initial dplyr data cleaning script
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
 create mode 100644 scripts/analysis.R
The output tells you:

main — the branch the commit was made on
root-commit — this is the very first commit (has no parent)
3f2a1b4 — the first 7 characters of the commit’s SHA-1 hash
1 file changed, 1 insertion(+) — what changed




Writing Research-Grade Commit Messages
A commit message is your lab notebook entry. Future you — and collaborators — will read these to understand what changed and why.
Format:
Short subject line (50 characters or less)

Optional body explaining WHY this change was made,
not what was changed (the diff shows that).
Wrap lines at 72 characters.
Examples using rehabilitation research context:







Bad message
Good message




fix
Fix: correct normalization formula for balance index


update analysis
Add baseline grip strength measurement function


changes
Data: exclude participant 42 — incomplete follow-up


wip
WIP: exploring two-way ANOVA for outcome measures


final
Manuscript: freeze analysis at Wave 1 submission version



Convention for this course: Start messages with a category word:

Add: — new functionality or content
Fix: — correcting an error
Data: — data management decisions
Docs: — documentation changes
Refactor: — restructuring without changing behaviour
WIP: — work-in-progress (clean up before merging)




git log: Reading History
git log: displays the commit history.
git log
commit 1a5f3b2c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a
Author: Your Name <your@institution.edu>
Date:   Thu Feb 28 09:30:00 2026 -0500

    Fix: correct normalization formula for balance index

commit 9d4c7e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0c1d2e3f4a5b6c7d
Author: Your Name <your@institution.edu>
Date:   Thu Feb 28 09:15:00 2026 -0500

    Add baseline grip strength measurement function

commit 3f2a1b4c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a
Author: Your Name <your@institution.edu>
Date:   Thu Feb 28 09:00:00 2026 -0500

    Add initial dplyr data cleaning script
Use flags to control the output:
git log --oneline          # one line per commit
1a5f3b2 Fix: correct normalization formula for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add baseline grip strength measurement function
3f2a1b4 Add initial dplyr data cleaning script
git log --oneline --graph --all   # ASCII graph of all branches
* 1a5f3b2 (HEAD -> main) Fix: correct normalization formula
* 9d4c7e1 Add baseline grip strength measurement function
* 3f2a1b4 Add initial dplyr data cleaning script



git diff: Comparing States
git diff: shows what changed between two states.
git diff                    # working directory vs staging area (unstaged changes)
git diff --staged           # staging area vs last commit (staged changes)
git diff HEAD~1 HEAD        # last commit vs the one before it
# After modifying analysis.R but before staging:
git diff
diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index a8c3d5e..f4e9b2c 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -1,2 +1,5 @@
 library(dplyr)
+
+# Normalize balance scores to 0-100 range
+normalize_balance <- function(x) {
+  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) * 100
+}
Reading diff output:

--- and +++ — the two versions being compared (before and after)
@@ — which lines are shown (context)
Lines starting with + — added lines (green in many terminals)
Lines starting with - — removed lines (red)
Lines with no prefix — unchanged context lines

title: Module 02: Basic Git Terminal Workflow
section: Before You Push: Data Safety Minimum
text:
Before You Push: Data Safety Minimum






Four Rules Before Any git push



Before connecting to a remote repository (Module 03), you must know these four rules. Violating them can expose participant data, credentials, or bloat your repository permanently.
Rule 1: Never commit files in data/ Create a .gitignore file with data/ as the first entry. Data files — even “synthetic” ones — should not be in version control by default.
Rule 2: Never commit .env or credential files API keys, passwords, and authentication tokens committed to Git are permanently accessible in history, even after deletion. Add .env to .gitignore immediately.
Rule 3: Never commit files containing participant identifiers or PHI De-identification does not protect against accidental commits. Use .gitignore to exclude all data folders. Module 08 covers this in full detail.
Rule 4: Always run git status before git push Check that no unexpected files are staged. Look at what you are about to share.
Quick .gitignore to create right now:
cat > .gitignore << 'EOF'
data/
.env
*.RData
*.csv
*.xlsx
EOF
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Add: initial .gitignore for research data"
Full data management coverage is in Module 08 — Data Management.

What’s Next

title: Module 04: Branching in the Terminal
text: Branching is Git’s superpower — it lets you develop a new analysis approach, fix a bug, or write documentation without touching your working main branch. In this module, you will see exactly how branches work under the hood, then create and switch between branches entirely in the terminal.

title: Module 04: Branching in the Terminal
text: Branching is Git’s superpower — it lets you develop a new analysis approach, fix a bug, or write documentation without touching your working main branch. In this module, you will see exactly how branches work under the hood, then create and switch between branches entirely in the terminal.

title: Module 04: Branching in the Terminal
text:
Prerequisites

Module 01 — Git Concepts (for understanding .git/refs/)
Module 03 — Remote Repositories



What Branches Really Are
Open a terminal in your repository and look at the branch files directly:
cd ~/rehab-research
cat .git/refs/heads/main
1a5f3b2c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a
That is the entire branch. A branch is a file containing one commit hash. Nothing more. When you make a new commit, Git rewrites that file with the new hash.

%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    C1["3f2a1b4\nInitial script"] --> C2["9d4c7e1\nAdd normalize"]
    C2 --> C3["1a5f3b2\nFix baseline"]

    MAIN["refs/heads/main\n= 1a5f3b2"] --> C3
    HEAD["HEAD\n= ref: refs/heads/main"] --> MAIN

    style MAIN fill:#50fa7b,color:#000
    style HEAD fill:#bd93f9,color:#000










When you create a new branch, Git creates a new file in refs/heads/ pointing to the same commit as your current branch. Branches are cheap because they are just a 41-byte text file.



Creating Branches
Three equivalent ways to create and switch to a new branch:
Modern syntax (recommended — Git ≥ 2.23):
git switch -c feature/add-grip-strength-measure
Legacy syntax (still widely used):
git checkout -b feature/add-grip-strength-measure
Two steps (create, then switch):
git branch feature/add-grip-strength-measure
git switch feature/add-grip-strength-measure
All three produce the same result. This course uses git switch (modern).
After creating:
cat .git/HEAD
ref: refs/heads/feature/add-grip-strength-measure
ls .git/refs/heads/
feature/  main
ls .git/refs/heads/feature/
add-grip-strength-measure
A new file appeared. It contains the same hash as main — branches start at the same point and diverge as you make new commits.



Switching Branches
Modern syntax:
git switch main           # switch back to main
git switch feature/add-grip-strength-measure   # switch to feature branch
Legacy syntax (still works):
git checkout main
When you switch branches, Git: 1. Updates HEAD to point to the new branch 2. Updates your working directory files to match the new branch’s latest commit






Uncommitted Changes Block Switching



If you have uncommitted changes when switching, Git will warn you:
error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by checkout:
        scripts/analysis.R
Either commit your changes first, or stash them (see Module 07 — Stashing).





Viewing Branches
git branch              # list local branches (* = current)
  feature/add-grip-strength-measure
* main
git branch -v           # show last commit on each branch
  feature/add-grip-strength-measure 1a5f3b2 Fix: correct normalization formula
* main                               1a5f3b2 Fix: correct normalization formula
git branch -a           # include remote-tracking branches
  feature/add-grip-strength-measure
* main
  remotes/origin/main



Branch Naming Conventions for Research
Good branch names are short, lower-case, hyphen-separated, and categorised:








Prefix
Use case
Example




feature/
New analysis feature or measure
feature/add-grip-strength-measure


fix/
Correcting a bug or error
fix/normalize-balance-scores


docs/
Documentation changes
docs/update-wave-2-protocol


data/
Data management work
data/exclude-incomplete-cases


refactor/
Code restructuring
refactor/modularize-analysis-functions


wip/
Early exploration
wip/anova-exploration



Avoid: spaces (use -), capitals (case-sensitive on Linux), very long names, and generic names like test or new.



Visualising Branch History
git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate
* 3c4d5e6 (HEAD -> feature/add-grip-strength-measure) Add: grip strength baseline function
* 1a5f3b2 (main, origin/main) Fix: correct normalization formula
* 9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
* 3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
After the feature branch diverges:
* 3c4d5e6 (HEAD -> feature/add-grip-strength-measure) Add: grip strength baseline function
| * 7e8f9a0 (main) Docs: update protocol for Wave 2
|/
* 1a5f3b2 Fix: correct normalization formula
* 9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
* 3f2a1b4 Add initial script
The | and / characters show the branch diverging and the * shows each commit.

title: Module 04: Branching in the Terminal

git branch -v

title: Module 06: Rebase via Terminal
text: Rebase is an alternative to merge that rewrites history to be linear. Instead of creating a merge commit, rebase “replays” your commits on top of another branch’s tip. The result looks as if you had started your work from the latest point in the target branch — no diamond shapes, no merge commits.

title: Module 06: Rebase via Terminal
text: Rebase is an alternative to merge that rewrites history to be linear. Instead of creating a merge commit, rebase “replays” your commits on top of another branch’s tip. The result looks as if you had started your work from the latest point in the target branch — no diamond shapes, no merge commits.

title: Module 06: Rebase via Terminal
text:
Prerequisites

Module 05 — Merging and Conflicts
At least one feature branch in ~/rehab-research/



git rebase: Moving Branch History
When you rebase feature onto main, Git:

Finds the common ancestor of both branches
Saves each commit on feature as a patch
Resets feature to point at main’s tip
Re-applies each patch in order

Before rebase:

%%{init: {'gitGraph': {'theme': 'dark'}} }%%
gitGraph
   commit id: "Initial"
   commit id: "Add normalize"
   branch feature/grip
   commit id: "Add grip fn"
   commit id: "Add tests"
   checkout main
   commit id: "Fix protocol"










After git rebase main on the feature branch:

%%{init: {'gitGraph': {'theme': 'dark'}} }%%
gitGraph
   commit id: "Initial"
   commit id: "Add normalize"
   commit id: "Fix protocol"
   branch feature/grip
   commit id: "Add grip fn (rebased)"
   commit id: "Add tests (rebased)"










The feature commits have new hashes (because their parent changed) but contain identical changes.



Merge vs Rebase: When to Use Each








Situation
Recommended
Reason




Bringing a personal feature branch up to date
git rebase main
Creates linear, readable history


Merging a completed feature into main
git merge (or PR)
Preserves the context of when feature was developed


Shared branch that others have pulled
Never rebase
Rewrites hashes — breaks teammates’ history


Cleaning up local WIP commits before sharing
git rebase -i
Squash into meaningful commits before pushing



Research context: Your personal analysis branches are safe to rebase. The main branch and any branch you have pushed and shared with a collaborator must never be rebased.



Basic Rebase: Updating a Feature Branch
To bring your feature branch up to date with main:
# Step 1: Update main
git switch main
git pull origin main

# Step 2: Switch to your feature branch
git switch feature/add-grip-strength-measure

# Step 3: Rebase onto main
git rebase main
If no conflicts:
Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/feature/add-grip-strength-measure.
Verify the result:
git log --oneline --graph --all
* 7e8f9a0 (HEAD -> feature/add-grip-strength-measure) Add: measure_grip_strength function
* 3c4d5e6 (main) Fix: correct protocol description
* 1a5f3b2 Add: normalize_balance function
* 9d4c7e1 Add: initial analysis script
A straight line — no merge commit needed.



Interactive Rebase: git rebase -i
Interactive rebase (-i) opens an editor showing the commits you are about to replay. You can reorder, edit, squash, or drop them.
git rebase -i HEAD~4
Nano (or your configured editor) opens with a todo list:
pick 3f2a1b4 WIP: starting normalize function
pick 9d4c7e1 WIP: still working on it
pick 1a5f3b2 maybe this works
pick a7b8c9d done with normalize

# Rebase 2e3f4a5..a7b8c9d onto 2e3f4a5 (4 commands)
#
# Commands:
# p, pick <commit> = use commit
# r, reword <commit> = use commit, but edit the commit message
# e, edit <commit> = use commit, but stop for amending
# s, squash <commit> = use commit, but meld into previous commit
# f, fixup <commit> = like "squash", but discard this commit's log message
# d, drop <commit> = remove commit
#
# If you remove a line, that commit is DROPPED.
# ...
Format of each line: command hash short-message
Available commands:



Command
Alias
What it does




pick
p
Keep the commit as-is


reword
r
Keep the commit but edit its message


edit
e
Pause rebase here to amend the commit


squash
s
Combine with previous commit; keep both messages


fixup
f
Combine with previous commit; discard this message


drop
d
Remove this commit entirely









Line Order = Commit Order



The topmost line = oldest commit (bottom of git log). The bottommost line = newest commit (top of git log). Reordering lines in the editor reorders commits in history.





Squashing Commits
Goal: Collapse 6 exploratory WIP commits into 2 meaningful research commits.
Before:
git log --oneline
f1e2d3c done
e2d3c4b getting closer
d3c4b5a maybe
c4b5a6d trying this
b5a6d7e still working
a6d7e8f WIP: add normalize function
Run interactive rebase:
git rebase -i HEAD~6
Editor opens. Change to:
pick a6d7e8f WIP: add normalize function
squash b5a6d7e still working
squash c4b5a6d trying this
squash d3c4b5a maybe
pick e2d3c4b getting closer
squash f1e2d3c done
Save and exit. Git applies the first group, then opens an editor for the combined commit message:
# This is a combination of 4 commits.
# This is the 1st commit message:

WIP: add normalize function

# This is the commit message #2:

still working
...
Delete everything and write a clean message:
Add: normalize_balance function with z-score and min-max implementations
Save. Git applies the second group (commits 5–6), opens another editor:
Add: validate normalize_balance against reference values
Save. Verify:
git log --oneline
9a8b7c6 Add: validate normalize_balance against reference values
4d5e6f7 Add: normalize_balance function with z-score and min-max implementations
Six commits became two — history is now lab-notebook quality.



Rewording Commit Messages
To change only the commit message without touching the content:
git rebase -i HEAD~3
Change pick to reword for the commits you want to rename:
reword 1a2b3c4 fix normalization
pick   5d6e7f8 Add: filter complete cases
reword 9a0b1c2 update analysis
Save. Git opens an editor for each reword commit separately. Write the improved message and save. After all rewording is done:
git log --oneline
3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization to match published protocol
5d6e7f8 Add: filter complete cases to exclude missing grip values
9a0b1c2 Update: refactor analysis script for readability



The Golden Rule of Rebase






Never Rebase Shared History



Once commits are on a remote branch that others have pulled, do not rebase them.
Why: Rebase creates new commit hashes. If a collaborator has the old hashes in their local repository, their history diverges from the rebased history. Git sees these as different commits and cannot automatically reconcile them. The result is a tangled history far worse than the WIP commits you were trying to clean up.
Safe to rebase: - Local branches you have never pushed - Branches you have pushed but no one else has pulled
Never rebase: - main / master - Any branch a collaborator has cloned or checked out - Any branch you have opened a pull request on





Rebase Conflicts
When replaying commits, a conflict can occur — the same resolution process as merge:
git rebase main
Auto-merging scripts/analysis.R
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in scripts/analysis.R
error: could not apply 3c4d5e6... Add: normalize function
hint: Resolve all conflicts manually, mark them as resolved with
hint: "git add/rm <conflicted_files>", then run "git rebase --continue".
hint: You can instead skip this commit: run "git rebase --skip".
hint: To abort and get back to the state before "git rebase", run "git rebase --abort".
Resolve:
# 1. Open and fix the conflict markers
nano scripts/analysis.R

# 2. Stage the resolved file
git add scripts/analysis.R

# 3. Continue replaying
git rebase --continue
Or abort to return to the pre-rebase state:
git rebase --abort
(silence = success)

title: Module 08: Data Management and .gitignore
text:
In rehabilitation research, version-controlled code sits alongside participant data, credential files, and large binary outputs. Git does not know which files are sensitive — you must tell it. This module covers .gitignore configuration, removing files that were committed accidentally, and auditing your repository before making it public.






Data Safety is Non-Negotiable



Every repository containing research code must have .gitignore configured before the first push. The cost of a mistake — accidentally committing participant IDs or API credentials — is irreversible once pushed to a public remote.

title: Module 08: Data Management and .gitignore
text:
In rehabilitation research, version-controlled code sits alongside participant data, credential files, and large binary outputs. Git does not know which files are sensitive — you must tell it. This module covers .gitignore configuration, removing files that were committed accidentally, and auditing your repository before making it public.






Data Safety is Non-Negotiable



Every repository containing research code must have .gitignore configured before the first push. The cost of a mistake — accidentally committing participant IDs or API credentials — is irreversible once pushed to a public remote.

title: Module 08: Data Management and .gitignore
text:
Prerequisites

Module 00 — Terminal Fundamentals
Module 01 — Git Concepts
Module 02 — Basic Workflow



Why .gitignore Matters for Research
Three scenarios where a missing or misconfigured .gitignore causes harm:
Scenario 1: Participant ID Exposure (PHI)
~/rehab-research/
├── scripts/
│   └── analysis.R
└── data/
    └── participants.csv    ← contains name, DOB, diagnosis codes
Without a data/ pattern in .gitignore, a git add . stages participants.csv. One git push to a public GitHub repository exposes protected health information. GitHub retains this in history even after deletion — a HIPAA breach that requires breach notification.
Scenario 2: Credential Leak
.env
.env files store API keys, database passwords, and OAuth tokens. A committed .env pushed to any remote (even private) creates a permanent record. Bots scan GitHub continuously for credential patterns and exploit leaked keys within minutes.
Scenario 3: Large File Bloat
data/wave1_mri_scans.nii.gz    ← 2.4 GB
Git stores every version of every file. A 2 GB file committed once inflates .git/objects/ permanently. Even after deletion, the file remains in history until a destructive git filter-repo run. The repository becomes impossible to clone over slow connections.



Creating .gitignore from the Terminal
Three methods — use whichever fits your workflow:
Method 1: nano (recommended for beginners)
nano .gitignore
Type patterns, one per line. Save: Ctrl+O, Return, Ctrl+X.
Method 2: Append a single pattern
echo 'data/' >> .gitignore
echo '*.RData' >> .gitignore
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
The >> operator appends to an existing file (or creates it). Safe to run multiple times — duplicates in .gitignore are harmless.
Method 3: Heredoc for multiple patterns at once
cat >> .gitignore << 'EOF'
data/
*.RData
*.csv
.env
.Rhistory
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.ipynb_checkpoints/
*.log
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
EOF
Verify the file was written:
cat .gitignore
data/
*.RData
*.csv
.env
.Rhistory
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.ipynb_checkpoints/
*.log
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db



Research-Specific .gitignore Patterns








Pattern
What it excludes
Why it matters for research




data/
Entire data directory
Keeps participant data and raw data out of version control


*.RData
R workspace snapshots
Can contain arbitrary objects including loaded data frames


*.csv
CSV files anywhere
Participant exports, survey data, exported results


.env
Environment variable file
API keys, database credentials, cloud storage tokens


.Rhistory
R console history
May contain column names, participant IDs typed interactively


__pycache__/
Python bytecode directory
Auto-generated, platform-specific, not reproducible across systems


*.pyc
Compiled Python files
Same reason as __pycache__/


.ipynb_checkpoints/
Jupyter notebook checkpoints
Auto-generated backup files with full notebook state including outputs


*.log
Log files
Analysis logs may contain participant identifiers or data summaries


Thumbs.db
Windows thumbnail cache
Binary, system-generated, no research value


.DS_Store
macOS folder metadata
Binary, system-generated, causes spurious diffs on cross-platform teams


figures/
Generated figure directory
If figures are generated by code, they should not be committed


renv/library/
renv package library
Large, platform-specific; renv.lock is sufficient for reproducibility









Directory vs File Patterns




data/ (trailing slash) → matches a directory named data and everything inside it
data (no slash) → matches a file OR directory named data
*.csv (glob) → matches any file ending in .csv in any subdirectory
data/*.csv → matches .csv files only inside the data/ directory

Use the trailing slash for directories to make your intent explicit.





Verifying .gitignore Works
Before and after comparison:
Before .gitignore:
mkdir -p data
echo "id,score" > data/results.csv
git status
On branch main
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        data/
        .env

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
After adding patterns to .gitignore:
echo 'data/' >> .gitignore
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
git status
On branch main
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        .gitignore

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
data/ and .env no longer appear. Git is ignoring them. Now commit .gitignore:
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Data: add .gitignore to exclude participant data and credentials"






.gitignore Only Works on Untracked Files



If a file has already been committed, adding its pattern to .gitignore has no effect. Git continues tracking the file. You must use git rm --cached to stop tracking it (see below).





git rm –cached: Removing Tracked Files
Scenario: You committed data/participants.csv before setting up .gitignore. Now you need to remove it from version control without deleting it from disk.
# Step 1: See the problem
git log --oneline -- data/participants.csv
3f2a1b4 Add: initial data file
# Step 2: Remove from Git's index (keep file on disk)
git rm --cached data/participants.csv
rm 'data/participants.csv'
# Step 3: Add the pattern to .gitignore so it won't be re-added
echo 'data/' >> .gitignore

# Step 4: Verify the file is still on disk
ls data/
participants.csv
# Step 5: Stage and commit the removal
git add .gitignore
git status
On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        deleted:    data/participants.csv
        modified:   .gitignore
git commit -m "Data: remove participant data from version control tracking"






The File Still Exists in History



git rm --cached removes the file from the current working tree and future commits. It does not remove the file from past commits. Anyone who clones the repository can access older commits containing the file.
If genuinely sensitive data was committed and pushed to a remote: 1. Immediately revoke any credentials and rotate API keys 2. Contact your institution’s data security office 3. Use git filter-repo (advanced — requires careful execution) to purge from history 4. Force-push the cleaned history to all remotes and notify all collaborators





Git LFS for Large Research Data Files
Git Large File Storage (LFS) is a Git extension for files too large to store in a standard Git repository (neuroimaging .nii.gz, video recordings, large tabular datasets).
# Step 1: Install Git LFS (macOS via Homebrew)
brew install git-lfs

# Step 2: Initialize LFS in your repository
git lfs install
Updated git hooks.
Git LFS initialized.
# Step 3: Track a file type
git lfs track "*.nii.gz"
Tracking "*.nii.gz"
This creates a .gitattributes file:
cat .gitattributes
*.nii.gz filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text
# Step 4: Commit the configuration
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "Data: configure Git LFS for neuroimaging files"

# Step 5: Verify tracking
git lfs ls-files
(empty — no .nii.gz files staged yet)






LFS Requires Remote Support



Git LFS stores file content on a separate LFS server, not in the standard Git objects. GitHub provides LFS storage (free tier: 1 GB, paid beyond). Your institution may have a self-hosted LFS server. Without LFS support on the remote, git push will fail with a message about missing LFS objects.
Alternative: Keep large raw data files outside the repository entirely and use data/ in .gitignore. Only commit derived outputs that fit within normal Git size limits.





Checking Repository Size
Monitor repository size before it becomes a problem:
git count-objects -vH
count: 42
size: 156.00 KiB
in-pack: 0
packs: 0
size-pack: 0 bytes
prune-packable: 0
garbage: 0
size-garbage: 0 bytes



Field
Meaning




count
Number of loose object files in .git/objects/


size
Total size of loose objects (human-readable with -H)


in-pack
Number of objects stored in pack files


packs
Number of pack files


size-pack
Total size of all pack files



A healthy research repository with only code and config files typically stays under 10 MB total. Alert thresholds:

> 50 MB: Investigate — likely binary files or data were committed
> 200 MB: Problematic for cloning; requires cleanup
> 1 GB: Critical; large files almost certainly in history

Find large objects:
git rev-list --objects --all | sort -k 2 > /tmp/git-objects.txt
git verify-pack -v .git/objects/pack/*.idx | sort -k 3 -n -r | head -20



Remediation Checklist
Run these commands before making any repository public:
# 1. Check repository size
git count-objects -vH

# 2. List all tracked files (should not include data/)
git ls-files | head -50

# 3. Search history for any CSV files ever committed
git log --all --full-history -- "*.csv"

# 4. Search history for any .env files ever committed
git log --all --full-history -- ".env"

# 5. Check for common PHI patterns in tracked file names
git ls-files | grep -iE "participant|subject|patient|id_|dob|phi"

# 6. Verify .gitignore excludes data directory
cat .gitignore | grep "^data"

# 7. Confirm no credential patterns in any tracked file
git grep -l "password\|api_key\|secret\|token" -- "*.R" "*.py" "*.md" 2>/dev/null

# 8. Verify .gitignore itself is committed
git log --oneline -- .gitignore
If any check reveals a problem, address it before pushing to a public remote.

title: Module 10: Troubleshooting and Recovery
text: Every Git user eventually makes a mistake: a commit with wrong content, an accidental git reset --hard, or confusion after detaching HEAD. Git’s recovery tools are surprisingly powerful — almost nothing is truly lost within the first 30 days. This module covers the full toolkit for undoing, recovering, and diagnosing problems.

title: Module 10: Troubleshooting and Recovery
text: Every Git user eventually makes a mistake: a commit with wrong content, an accidental git reset --hard, or confusion after detaching HEAD. Git’s recovery tools are surprisingly powerful — almost nothing is truly lost within the first 30 days. This module covers the full toolkit for undoing, recovering, and diagnosing problems.

title: Module 10: Troubleshooting and Recovery
text:
Prerequisites

Module 02 — Basic Workflow
Module 01 — Git Concepts (for understanding objects and refs)



Reading Git Error Messages
Git messages have four severity levels:








Prefix
Severity
Meaning




fatal:
Highest
Operation could not start or complete — Git stopped entirely


error:
High
An operation failed; Git may have partially applied changes


warning:
Medium
Something unusual happened; operation continued


hint:
Informational
Git suggestion — often tells you exactly how to fix the problem



Strategy: Always read hint: lines first — Git frequently tells you the fix directly.
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not
hint: have locally. Integrate the remote changes (e.g.
hint: 'git pull --rebase ...') before pushing again.
The fix is in the hint: git pull --rebase.



Undoing Working Directory Changes: git restore
Discard unstaged changes to a file — restore it to its last committed state:
git restore scripts/analysis.R
Discard all unstaged changes across all files:
git restore .






git restore Is Permanent for Unstaged Changes



Changes discarded with git restore cannot be recovered. They were never in a commit or the stash, so Git has no record of them. Double-check that you do not need the changes before running this command.


Restore a file to how it looked in a specific commit:
git restore --source 3c4d5e6 scripts/analysis.R
This does not change the commit history — it just updates your working directory. The file will appear as “modified” in git status.



Undoing Staged Changes: git restore –staged
Move a staged file back to the working directory (unstage it):
git restore --staged scripts/analysis.R
(no output — the file is back in working directory, unstaged)
Verify:
git status
On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
The change is still in your working directory — you just removed it from the staging area. The file is not lost.



Undoing Commits: git reset
git reset moves the current branch pointer backwards. Three modes control what happens to the changes from the undone commits:



Mode
Branch pointer
Staging area
Working directory




--soft
Moved back
Keeps changes staged
Unchanged


--mixed (default)
Moved back
Unstages changes
Unchanged


--hard
Moved back
Cleared
Changes discarded



–soft: Undo commit, keep changes staged
git reset HEAD~1 --soft
The last commit is undone. All its changes are staged, ready to recommit. Use this to fix a commit message or split a commit.
–mixed: Undo commit, keep changes in working directory
git reset HEAD~1
(Default mode — --mixed is implied.) The commit is undone. Changes are in your working directory but not staged. Use this to reorganize which files go into a commit.
–hard: Undo commit, discard all changes
git reset HEAD~1 --hard






–hard Discards Work Permanently (Usually)



git reset --hard discards the changes from the undone commits. They are not staged, not in the working directory, and not in any commit.
However: If the discarded commits were already committed (they were reachable from some branch), they are still in .git/objects/ and retrievable via git reflog for approximately 30 days until garbage collection.
Use --hard only when you are certain you want to discard the changes.





git reflog: Your Safety Net
The reflog records every movement of HEAD — every commit, reset, checkout, and branch switch. It is your recovery tool for almost any mistake.
git reflog
7e8f9a0 (HEAD -> main) HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
3c4d5e6 HEAD@{1}: commit: Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 HEAD@{2}: commit: Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 HEAD@{3}: commit: Add: initial analysis script
1a5f3b2 HEAD@{4}: checkout: moving from feature/grip to main
Each entry shows: hash (ref) HEAD@{N}: action: description
HEAD@{0} is the current position. HEAD@{1} is where HEAD was before the last action.
Recover a “lost” commit after git reset --hard:
# Identify the hash of the commit you want to recover
git reflog
# Find the hash — e.g., 3c4d5e6

# Option 1: Check it out to verify the content
git checkout 3c4d5e6
# (you are now in detached HEAD — see below)

# Option 2: Create a branch at that commit immediately
git switch -c recovered-work 3c4d5e6



Detached HEAD: Understanding and Fixing
“Detached HEAD” means HEAD points directly to a commit hash rather than to a branch name.
How it happens:
git checkout 3c4d5e6       # checking out a commit hash
git checkout v1.0.0        # checking out a tag
gh pr checkout 47          # checking out a PR
What git status shows:
git status
HEAD detached at 3c4d5e6
nothing to commit, working tree clean
What it means: You can look at files and even make commits — but those commits are not on any branch. As soon as you switch to another branch, they become unreachable (though still recoverable via reflog for ~30 days).
Fix 1: Go back to a branch without saving anything
git switch main
Fix 2: Save commits made in detached HEAD onto a new branch
# (while still in detached HEAD, after making some commits)
git switch -c rescue-branch
Now your commits are safely on rescue-branch and HEAD is attached.



Recovering Lost Commits
Full recovery scenario:
# You ran git reset --hard one too many times
git log --oneline
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script
The “Fix: use z-score normalization” commit is gone from git log. Recover it:
git reflog
9d4c7e1 (HEAD -> main) HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
3c4d5e6 HEAD@{1}: commit: Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 HEAD@{2}: commit: Add: normalize_balance function
# The lost commit hash is 3c4d5e6
git switch -c recovery-branch 3c4d5e6
Switched to a new branch 'recovery-branch'
git log --oneline
3c4d5e6 (HEAD -> recovery-branch) Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script
Recovered. Now merge or cherry-pick as needed.



git fsck: Repository Health Check
git fsck verifies the integrity of the Git object database:
git fsck
Checking object directories: 100% (256/256), done.
Checking connectivity: done.
If there are problems:
dangling commit 7e8f9a0b1c2d3e4f5a6b7c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f
dangling tree 1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b
Dangling objects are commits or trees not reachable from any branch or tag — often created by git reset or rebases. They are harmless and will be garbage collected eventually.
With --lost-found:
git fsck --lost-found
Unreachable objects are written to .git/lost-found/. You can inspect them with git show.



Resetting to Remote State
When your local branch has diverged from the remote and you want to discard all local changes:






This Discards All Local Commits



The following command permanently discards any local commits that are not on the remote. Make sure you have pushed or stashed anything you want to keep.


git fetch origin
git reset --hard origin/main
HEAD is now at 7e8f9a0 Fix: update inclusion criteria for Wave 2
Your local main now exactly matches the remote.
Nuclear option — destroy and re-clone:
cd ..
rm -rf rehab-research
git clone git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git






rm -rf Destroys Everything Permanently



This deletes the entire repository including .git/. All local commits not pushed, all stashes, all reflog history — everything is gone. Only use this if the repository is completely corrupted and you have confirmed everything important is on the remote.

This module covers the structure, documentation, and release process that transform a working Git repository into a citable, reproducible research artifact.

title: Module 12: Professional Research Repositories
text:
Prerequisites

Module 03 — Remote Repositories
Module 08 — Data Management (data safety practices)



Research Repository Structure
A well-structured repository signals professionalism and aids reproducibility:
tree ~/rehab-research
rehab-research/
├── README.md               ← project overview, installation, usage, citation
├── LICENSE                 ← how others may use your code and data
├── .gitignore              ← excludes data/, credentials, system files
├── renv.lock               ← (R projects) locked package versions
├── scripts/
│   ├── analysis.R          ← main analysis functions
│   ├── figures.R           ← figure generation
│   └── preprocessing.R     ← data cleaning (operates on data/ locally)
├── docs/
│   ├── protocol.md         ← study protocol and pre-registration details
│   └── codebook.md         ← variable definitions and measurement descriptions
├── figures/                ← gitignored — generated by scripts/figures.R
│   └── .gitkeep            ← empty file to preserve directory in repository
└── data/                   ← gitignored — never committed
    └── .gitkeep            ← preserves directory structure for collaborators
Why .gitkeep? An empty directory cannot be committed (Git tracks files, not directories). An empty .gitkeep file preserves the directory structure so collaborators know where to put their local data files after cloning.
touch figures/.gitkeep data/.gitkeep
git add figures/.gitkeep data/.gitkeep
git commit -m "Structure: add gitkeep files to preserve empty directories"



Writing README.md from the Terminal
A README answers five questions any reader will have:
nano README.md
Template (fill in for your project):
# Grip Strength and Balance Outcomes in Stroke Rehabilitation

Analysis code for the study: "Recovery trajectories of grip strength and postural
balance in adults following first-time stroke." [Journal, Year, DOI when available]

## Installation

```bash
git clone git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
cd rehab-research
Rscript -e "renv::restore()"   # installs all R packages from renv.lock

title: Module 12: Professional Research Repositories
text:
Usage
Data files are not included in this repository (participant privacy). Place your data files in data/ following the codebook in docs/codebook.md.
Run the analysis:
Rscript scripts/preprocessing.R   # cleans raw data → data/processed/
Rscript scripts/analysis.R        # runs main analysis → output/results.csv
Rscript scripts/figures.R         # generates figures → figures/

title: Module 12: Professional Research Repositories
text:
License
Code: MIT License (see LICENSE file). Data: CC BY 4.0 (see data archive).

Save and commit:

```bash
git add README.md
git commit -m "Docs: add README with installation, usage, and citation"


Choosing a License
Research code and content have different licensing norms:








License
Best for
Key permission




MIT
Analysis scripts, R functions, Python code
Use, modify, distribute freely; attribution required


Apache 2.0
Software tools with patent considerations
Same as MIT + explicit patent grant


GPL-3.0
Code built on GPL-licensed tools
Derivatives must also be open source


CC BY 4.0
Documentation, data, figures
Use freely with attribution


CC BY-NC 4.0
Content you want to restrict commercially
Non-commercial use only



For most analysis code, MIT is the appropriate choice. Add it:
cat > LICENSE << 'EOF'
MIT License

Copyright (c) 2026 Dr. J. Bayer

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
copies or substantial portions of the Software.

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.
EOF

git add LICENSE
git commit -m "Legal: add MIT license for analysis code"



Semantic Versioning via git tag
Version your analysis code using MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH conventions:








Version bump
When
Example change




MAJOR
Breaking change to analysis methodology
Switch from min-max to z-score normalization


MINOR
New measure or function added
Add grip strength asymmetry index


PATCH
Bug fix, clarification, typo
Fix variable name in codebook



Tag the first public release:
# Final cleanup before tagging
git log --oneline
git status
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Analysis for Wave 1 manuscript submission (Smith et al., 2026)"
Verify:
git show v1.0.0
tag v1.0.0
Tagger: Dr. J. Bayer <j.bayer@rehab-institute.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 28 16:00:00 2026 +0100

Analysis for Wave 1 manuscript submission (Smith et al., 2026)

commit 3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b1c2 (HEAD -> main, tag: v1.0.0)
...
Push the tag:
git push origin v1.0.0
Total 0 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
 * [new tag]         v1.0.0 -> v1.0.0



Archiving for Publication: git archive
Create a clean snapshot of the repository at a specific tag — no .git/ history, no ignored files — as a zip archive for manuscript submission or data repository deposit:
git archive --format=zip v1.0.0 > rehab-research-v1.0.0.zip
Verify the contents:
unzip -l rehab-research-v1.0.0.zip | head -20
Archive:  rehab-research-v1.0.0.zip
  Length      Date    Time    Name
---------  ---------- -----   ----
      523  02-28-2026 16:00   README.md
     1084  02-28-2026 16:00   LICENSE
       89  02-28-2026 16:00   .gitignore
     2341  02-28-2026 16:00   scripts/analysis.R
     1102  02-28-2026 16:00   scripts/figures.R
      467  02-28-2026 15:30   docs/protocol.md
...
The archive contains only committed, tracked files at the v1.0.0 state. Data files in data/ are not included (they are gitignored).



Creating a GitHub Release
Make the tag visible as a formal release on GitHub:
gh release create v1.0.0 \
  --title "Wave 1 Analysis — Manuscript Submission" \
  --notes "Analysis code accompanying: Bayer et al. (2026) *Journal Name*.

**What's included**: All analysis scripts, codebook, and protocol documentation.

**Data**: Raw participant data are not included per IRB data management plan.
Request access through the institutional data archive (DOI: XXXXX)." \
  rehab-research-v1.0.0.zip
https://github.com/yourusername/rehab-research/releases/tag/v1.0.0
The release page on GitHub now shows the release notes and the zip archive as a downloadable asset. This URL is citable and persistent.



Publication Readiness Terminal Checklist
Run all eight checks before pushing to a public remote or submitting to a journal:
# 1. Check repository size
echo "=== Repository size ===" && git count-objects -vH

# 2. Confirm .gitignore excludes data directory
echo "=== .gitignore data check ===" && grep "^data" .gitignore && echo "PASS: data/ excluded" || echo "FAIL: no data/ pattern found"

# 3. List all currently tracked files (check for unexpected data files)
echo "=== All tracked files ===" && git ls-files

# 4. Search history for any CSV files ever committed
echo "=== CSV history ===" && git log --all --full-history -- "*.csv" | head -5

# 5. Check for any .env files ever committed
echo "=== .env history ===" && git log --all --full-history -- ".env" | head -5

# 6. Verify README exists and is committed
echo "=== README check ===" && git ls-files README.md && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL: no README.md"

# 7. Verify LICENSE exists and is committed
echo "=== LICENSE check ===" && git ls-files LICENSE && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL: no LICENSE"

# 8. Verify version tag exists
echo "=== Tags ===" && git tag
All eight commands should return “PASS” or expected content. Any failures indicate a gap to address before publication.
